title: SSR-safe useMediaQuery hook with no hydration mismatch and modern addEventListener API
viewport_spectrum: All viewports — 320px Galaxy S8 portrait, 375px iPhone SE, 768px iPad portrait, 1024px iPad landscape, 1280px MacBook Air, 1920px desktop, 3840px 4K. Must also handle Next.js SSR where server has no window object.
scenario_context: A Next.js 15 SaaS dashboard renders a sidebar on desktop and a bottom nav on mobile. The current implementation uses `window.innerWidth < 768` in render which causes hydration errors and a layout flash. The team needs a reusable hook that is SSR-safe, doesn't cause layout shift, listens for viewport changes with the modern API, and supports multiple simultaneous breakpoints without each hook adding its own listener.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Calling window.innerWidth in the initial render (hydration mismatch)
Using window.matchMedia(q).addListener (deprecated, breaks in Safari 14+)
Returning false during SSR and true on client without suppressHydrationWarning strategy
Creating one MediaQueryList per component instance (memory leak in large trees)
useEffect that sets state synchronously without checking matches first
responsive_reasoning:
1. The fundamental change between viewports here is not size but COMPONENT IDENTITY. The sidebar component and the bottom nav are different React trees with different a11y semantics — this is adaptive, not responsive. So accurate detection is load-bearing.
2. This needs architecture because every component asking 'am I mobile?' creates a new MediaQueryList. With 50 components on a page, that's 50 listeners firing on every resize. Centralize via context or a singleton registry.
3. Input model differences are downstream of detection — once you know 'touch + small viewport', you switch to bottom nav with thumb-zone optimized targets. Detection itself is input-agnostic.
4. Performance: matchMedia is native and fires only at boundary crossings, not on every pixel. ResizeObserver fires continuously. For breakpoint detection, matchMedia wins.
5. Accessibility: avoid rendering BOTH layouts and hiding one with CSS — that double-renders to screen readers. Truly swap based on detection.
6. Edge cases: iOS Safari's address bar collapse changes innerHeight by ~85px but does NOT fire a media query on (height:...). The viewport API is the right tool.
7. Rejected: react-responsive (subscribes per-component, no SSR strategy). Rejected: CSS-only (loses ability to render different React trees with different ARIA).

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport width breakpoint crossings, with explicit SSR fallback
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: window.matchMedia + MediaQueryList.addEventListener('change', ...)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Initial render returns the SSR-default value (caller decides). useSyncExternalStore guarantees client snapshot is read AFTER hydration, so server HTML matches initial client render. Subsequent paint reflects real viewport.
detection_strategy.code_pattern: const subscribe = (query: string) => (cb: () => void) => { const mql = window.matchMedia(query); mql.addEventListener('change', cb); return () => mql.removeEventListener('change', cb); };

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Hook-driven component swap (adaptive), CSS for fluid sizing within each layout
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Hard breakpoint at 768px because the component tree differs. Within each tree, clamp() for spacing.
layout_strategy.content_priority: Identical information density — bottom nav surfaces the same 5 destinations as sidebar, just spatially reorganized.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: None — this is the rare case where 'adaptive swap' is correct because keyboard/screen-reader semantics differ between sidebar and bottom nav.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Bottom nav with 56pt tall items, thumb-zone optimized, haptic on tap via Vibration API where supported
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Sidebar rail (icons only) at 768-1023, full sidebar at 1024+
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Full sidebar with hover states, keyboard shortcuts displayed on hover
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab order: sidebar → main content → inspector. Arrow keys within sidebar navigate items.

performance_notes: useSyncExternalStore reads ONCE per render, no re-subscription per change. matchMedia listeners are native and fire only at boundary crossings, not on every resize pixel. SSR cookie hint prevents layout flash for returning users — first-time visitors get one paint of the default layout, acceptable trade-off. No window.innerWidth in render path = no forced layout recalc.
accessibility_across_viewports: Sidebar uses role=\"navigation\" aria-label=\"Primary\" with role=\"menuitem\" children. BottomNav uses the same role but aria-label=\"Primary mobile navigation\" so screen reader users know context changed. Touch targets in BottomNav are 56pt (exceeds 44pt minimum). Focus order is preserved across layout swap because semantic order matches in both trees.
edge_cases_handled: SSR returns wrong layout briefly for users without cookie — single paint, no hydration error because useSyncExternalStore guarantees match
User rotates iPad from portrait (768) to landscape (1024) — sidebar transforms rail → full without remount because both render <Sidebar variant=...> branch
User in iPad split-view shrinks effective viewport to 320px — matchMedia fires immediately, swaps to mobile layout
Browser zoom 200% — media queries fire in CSS pixels so 1280px CSS → still desktop at 200% zoom even if physical pixels say otherwise (correct behavior)
User has reduced data mode and disables JS — server-rendered default layout works because no JS-only interactions
what_breaks_naive_approach: The `window.innerWidth < 768` in render fails SSR (no window) and creates a hydration mismatch because the server renders the desktop tree and the client immediately renders mobile. React 19 will throw a hydration error and tear down the entire subtree to recover, causing a visible layout shift and resetting any focused inputs or scroll positions in the affected subtree.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) useSyncExternalStore is the only correct primitive for 'derive React state from a browser API that exists outside React' — it solves hydration safely. 2) Cookie-based SSR layout hint means returning users get the right layout on first paint. 3) The hook returns a string-keyed breakpoint, not booleans, so the consumer can exhaustively switch and the type system catches missing cases.

title: Product card that adapts to its container (sidebar, grid, hero) — not the viewport
viewport_spectrum: Card rendered at 240px (narrow sidebar slot), 360px (mobile full-width), 480px (2-up grid on tablet), 720px (single-column feature row), 1200px (full-bleed hero). The viewport is irrelevant — the container drives layout.
scenario_context: An e-commerce site has a ProductCard rendered in 6 different contexts: the recommendation sidebar, the search results grid, the cart drawer, the order confirmation page, the related-products carousel, and the hero feature slot. Today there are 6 forked components because media queries can't tell 'narrow sidebar at 1920px desktop' apart from 'mobile full-width at 375px' — both might be 280px wide.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Forking the component per slot (maintenance nightmare)
Using media queries on viewport width (wrong — container width is what matters)
Reading container size via ResizeObserver and setting state (re-renders, layout thrash)
Hardcoded breakpoints that ignore typography and image needs
Forgetting to set container-type which makes @container rules silent no-ops
responsive_reasoning:
1. What changes is the COMPOSITION: at 240px the card is image-only with overlay text; at 480px it splits to image-left/text-right; at 720px it becomes a horizontal card with image, text, AND a description column.
2. Architecture-not-styling: container queries push layout decisions to the leaf component, so the SAME <ProductCard /> works in any slot without prop drilling 'variant' enums. Slots just provide width.
3. Input model is constant (touch or mouse — card is just a link), but tap target sizes need to scale with the card.
4. Performance: container queries are evaluated by the browser layout engine, no JS, no observers. Pure CSS, GPU-friendly.
5. Accessibility: regardless of layout, the card is one focusable link with the same accessible name. Visual reorganization doesn't change tab order.
6. Edge cases: nested container queries (card-in-card), subgrid for aligned rows, fallback for browsers without container query support.
7. Rejected: useResizeObserver + JS-driven className — re-renders on every resize, breaks during scroll-driven sizing changes. Rejected: viewport media queries — they have no idea how wide the actual slot is.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Container inline-size (width)
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS container-type: inline-size + @container queries
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure CSS — works identically on server and client. No JS = no hydration concerns.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS container queries with named containers + CSS Grid for internal layout
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Container breakpoints at 320, 480, 720 — but image aspect-ratio and typography use clamp() within each band for smooth scaling.
layout_strategy.content_priority: Always shown: image, title, price. At >=480px: rating. At >=720px: description excerpt + 'add to cart' button inline.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Nothing is HIDDEN — at small sizes, secondary info goes under primary; at large sizes it goes beside. Same information graph, different spatial arrangement.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Entire card is tap target (44pt minimum enforced via clamp on padding)
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Hover state appears via (hover: hover) media query — touchscreens skip it
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover reveals quick-add button, cursor changes to pointer
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Single tab stop per card, Enter opens detail. Quick-add accessible via separate focusable button at larger container sizes.

performance_notes: Zero JS for layout. Container queries are CSS-native and computed during layout pass. cqi (container query inline) units mean typography and spacing scale smoothly without any media query boundaries. Image sizes attribute uses cqi too so the browser requests the right resolution based on container, not viewport — critical for sidebar slots where the image is small even on desktop.
accessibility_across_viewports: One focusable link per card regardless of layout. The 'Add' button at large sizes is a SEPARATE focusable element with its own accessible name. Star rating has aria-label so screen readers don't read '★ 4.5 (120)' as 'star 4.5 paren 120'. Touch targets enforce min-h-11 (44px) on interactive buttons.
edge_cases_handled: Card rendered in a 200px sidebar at 1920px desktop — collapses to image+title only
Card rendered in a 720px hero slot at 375px mobile (impossible in practice but layout still works)
Nested cards (carousel of cards inside a card) — named container 'card' prevents accidental matching
Browser without container query support (Firefox <110 in legacy environments) — @supports fallback shows stacked layout
Image still loading — aspect-square reservation prevents layout shift
what_breaks_naive_approach: Viewport media queries see '1920px desktop' and render the wide layout, even though the actual card is in a 240px sidebar. The image is now 240px wide but the browser downloaded the 1200px version. Text is too big for the slot, truncation kicks in, the card looks broken. Container queries solve this fundamentally — the card knows its OWN width, not the window's.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same component, six contexts, zero forks — the card is genuinely context-aware. 2) cqi units in clamp() means typography scales with the container smoothly between breakpoints, not in steps. 3) sizes attribute uses cqi so the browser requests appropriately-sized images per slot, not per viewport — a sidebar card on 4K downloads a small image.

title: Fluid type scale with clamp(), accessible minimums, and proper line-length constraints
viewport_spectrum: 320px iPhone SE, 768px iPad, 1280px laptop, 1920px desktop, 3840px 4K. Plus 200% browser zoom (effective 640px for 1280px screen) and user-set base font 24px (1.5x default).
scenario_context: A long-form blog/documentation site. Body text must be readable at every viewport without zooming. Headings need to scale meaningfully — not just 'bigger' but proportionally to maintain hierarchy. The current design uses Tailwind's text-base / text-lg / text-xl with hard breakpoints, causing jarring 30% size jumps at 768px and 1024px. Users with 200% zoom find the line length too short to read.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed px font sizes (breaks user font preferences)
Tailwind responsive prefixes for type (text-base md:text-lg lg:text-xl creates steps not flow)
clamp() with viewport-only middle term (1vw makes text shrink at narrow viewports below minimum)
max-width in px for prose (doesn't adapt to base font size)
Line-height as a unitless ratio that doesn't account for varying font sizes per heading
responsive_reasoning:
1. Fundamental change: at 320px text needs to be readable while leaving room for a small word count per line. At 1920px text can be larger but line length must stay readable (~65ch). Two different optimization targets, continuous between them.
2. Architecture: type scale is a SYSTEM, not per-component decisions. Define once in CSS custom properties, consume everywhere. Adding a new heading level shouldn't require updating 50 components.
3. Input model: irrelevant for typography itself, but mobile-touch users often scroll fast and need higher contrast/larger sizes to skim.
4. Performance: pure CSS clamp() is computed at layout time. No JS, no re-renders. Use system fonts for fastest paint, web fonts via font-display: swap for late enhancement.
5. Accessibility: minimum size must be >= 16px CSS pixels for body (browser default). Users at 200% zoom will get 32px effective — design must not break. rem units honor user-set base size.
6. Edge cases: very long words (URLs, German compounds) need overflow-wrap: anywhere. RTL languages may need different scale.
7. Rejected: utopia.fyi static export — fine as a starting point but doesn't compose with container queries. Rejected: media-query-based steps — visible jumps.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport width (for clamp middle term), user-set base font size (implicit via rem)
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS clamp() with vw + rem combination, rem units throughout
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure CSS — identical on server and client.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS custom properties + clamp() type scale, ch units for line-length
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: 100% fluid — zero breakpoints in the type system. Headings scale continuously.
layout_strategy.content_priority: Text content is the priority. Layout serves text legibility.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Line length stays ~45-75ch across all viewports — that's the constraint that drives container width, not the other way around.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Larger tap targets for inline links (padding around them), but text size itself follows the scale
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same scale, optimal reading width matches the viewport naturally
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover underline on links, cursor changes — text otherwise identical
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Focus rings visible on links, no impact on type

performance_notes: Pure CSS — no JS overhead. System fonts mean no font-loading flash. clamp() is computed at layout time and cached. Browsers optimize for repeated rem calculations. Zero runtime cost beyond initial layout pass.
accessibility_across_viewports: Minimum body size is 16px (1rem at default browser setting). At 200% zoom, body becomes 32px effective — clamp middle term continues to scale upward but minimum holds floor. Users with custom base font size of 20px get a proportionally larger scale — the entire system scales because everything is rem-based. Line length capped at 65ch keeps reading effortless at any size. Letter-spacing tightens at larger headings to prevent over-spaced display type, and stays neutral at body sizes.
edge_cases_handled: User at 200% browser zoom — text scales up, container stays readable
User with custom base font size 24px — entire scale shifts proportionally
Very long URL in prose — overflow-wrap: anywhere prevents horizontal scroll
Heading at end of narrow column — clamp prevents h1 from being unreadably small at 320px
Print stylesheet (not shown) — can override with fixed pt sizes if needed
User with prefers-reduced-data — system fonts only, no web font download
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tailwind responsive type (text-base md:text-lg lg:text-2xl) creates VISIBLE jumps at 768px and 1024px — users dragging a window or rotating their tablet see text snap to a different size. Even worse, fixed px sizes ignore the user's browser font preference entirely — a user who set 20px base because they have low vision still gets your 14px text. The clamp() approach honors user preferences AND scales smoothly.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Zero breakpoints in the type system — completely fluid, no visible jumps. 2) Minimums enforced via clamp() lower bound, so accessibility doesn't degrade at narrow viewports. 3) ch-based measure means line length adapts to font size, not viewport — print-quality typography that's automatic.

title: Spatial scale with clamp() for padding, margin, and gap that maintains rhythm across viewports
viewport_spectrum: 320px to 3840px continuous. Particularly tested at 375px (iPhone), 414px (iPhone Plus), 768px (iPad portrait), 1024px (iPad landscape), 1280px (laptop), 1920px (desktop), 2560px (QHD), 3840px (4K).
scenario_context: A marketing site uses Tailwind's spacing scale (p-4 md:p-8 lg:p-12 xl:p-16) and looks great at exactly those breakpoints. Between them it looks awkward — 769px has the same generous padding as 1024px, and 1281px has the same padding as 1919px. Designer wants spacing to feel proportional at every viewport, with a system that maps to her Figma spacing variables.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tailwind responsive padding prefixes that create discrete jumps
vw-based spacing (collapses to 0 at narrow viewports, runaway at wide)
rem-only spacing (doesn't adapt to viewport at all)
Different spacing scales per component (loss of rhythm)
Negative space that scales down faster than content (content overflows)
responsive_reasoning:
1. Fundamental change: at 320px, 16px padding consumes 10% of viewport; at 1920px the same 16px consumes <1%. Visually unbalanced. Spacing should feel CONSISTENT proportionally, not numerically.
2. Architecture: define spacing tokens once via CSS custom properties, consume in Tailwind via arbitrary values or extended theme. Single source of truth.
3. Input model: touch needs more spacing for fat-finger forgiveness. Mouse can use tighter spacing.
4. Performance: pure CSS, evaluated at layout time, no JS.
5. Accessibility: spacing affects readability and tap targets. Maintaining >=44pt around interactive elements is non-negotiable.
6. Edge cases: nested spacing (a section padded inside a section padded inside a container) compounds — caps via min() prevent runaway.
7. Rejected: hard breakpoints with arbitrary values per breakpoint — works for one design but doesn't compose.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport width (implicitly via vw in clamp)
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS custom properties with clamp()
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure CSS — identical on both.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS custom properties + clamp() with min() caps
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Continuous fluid with min/max bounds. Zero breakpoints.
layout_strategy.content_priority: Content density stays consistent — proportional whitespace at every viewport.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Density visually constant. Spacing scales proportional to viewport.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Larger gap between interactive elements (--space-lg minimum 12px between buttons)
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same scale, more breathing room appears naturally
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Same scale, tighter feel because elements aren't proportionally as large
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Focus rings need 4px clear space — accounted for in --space-xs minimum

performance_notes: Pure CSS, computed at layout time. clamp() values are cached per viewport width. No JS, no observers, no re-renders. Custom properties resolve at use site with no perf penalty vs literal values.
accessibility_across_viewports: Interactive element spacing (--space-sm minimum 16px) ensures tap targets don't crowd each other. Focus rings (default 4px offset) get clearance via --space-xs minimum 12px. At 200% zoom, spacing scales because rem honors user font size. Touch users on small phones get the lower bound of the scale, which is still adequate.
edge_cases_handled: Nested sections — outer padding + inner padding doesn't compound to runaway space (caps prevent it)
4K monitor — container padding caps at 4rem, prevents 200px gutters
320px Galaxy Fold closed — minimums ensure content has breathing room
User zooms to 200% — rem-based scale shifts up proportionally
Print stylesheet — can override with cm-based units
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tailwind responsive prefixes (p-4 md:p-8 lg:p-12) create visible jumps. At 767px the padding is 16px; at 768px it becomes 32px. The user dragging a window sees a sudden visual snap. With clamp(), the same range sees padding smoothly grow from ~17px to ~24px. The fluid approach is invisible to users — exactly the point.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Zero breakpoints in spacing — fully fluid. 2) Caps via clamp() upper bound prevent 4K monitors from looking comical. 3) Same token system maps to Figma variables, so design-to-code is lossless.

title: Pointer-aware hover affordances that don't break touch users
viewport_spectrum: Any device with primary input variation: iPhone (touch only), iPad with Magic Keyboard trackpad (hybrid), Surface Pro with pen+touch+mouse, MacBook (mouse/trackpad), MacBook with touchscreen (rare but exists). Desktop in tablet mode on Windows hybrids.
scenario_context: A photo gallery shows hover overlays (caption + actions) on desktop. On touch devices the overlay is permanently visible (overwhelming) OR requires a long-press that conflicts with iOS's image save gesture. The team needs a pattern that uses hover when hover is available, and a different affordance when it isn't — including hybrid devices that have BOTH.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Detecting touch via 'ontouchstart' in window (Surface Pro fails)
User agent sniffing for iOS/Android (misses iPad-as-desktop mode)
Using :hover without @media (hover: hover) wrapper (sticks on touch)
Long-press as the only mobile alternative (conflicts with system gestures, no discoverability)
Treating hybrid devices as one-or-the-other
responsive_reasoning:
1. Fundamental change: hover is a STATE that exists between 'not interacting' and 'committed action'. Touch has no such state. Mouse has cursor precision (sub-pixel); touch has 44pt minimum. These are different paradigms.
2. Architecture: a hover-revealed overlay must have a touch alternative — either always-visible action button, or tap-to-reveal-then-tap-to-act two-step. Decide once, apply consistently.
3. Input model is THE topic here — every component that uses hover needs an explicit touch story.
4. Performance: pointer media queries are CSS-native, zero JS overhead. JS detection via PointerEvent.pointerType is event-time only.
5. Accessibility: keyboard users need :focus-visible parity with hover state. Screen reader users get always-revealed text via aria-describedby.
6. Edge cases: hybrid devices (iPad + trackpad) — (hover: hover) is true when trackpad attached, false when detached. Component should react.
7. Rejected: 'mobile is touch, desktop is mouse' — wrong. Surface Pro is desktop AND touch. iPad with keyboard is mobile AND mouse-equivalent.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Primary pointer type and hover capability
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: @media (hover: hover) and (pointer: fine) — CSS. Per-event detection via PointerEvent.pointerType for JS-needed cases.
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: CSS-based detection works on server (renders both states, CSS picks). For JS-only paths, default to no-hover behavior on server.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS pointer media queries + always-visible action button as touch fallback
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Pointer media queries are categorical, not fluid. The breakpoint IS the input change.
layout_strategy.content_priority: On hover-capable: caption appears on hover, action button on hover. On touch: caption always visible (smaller), action button always visible (top-right corner).
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Touch shows more by default (no hover state to reveal info); hover-capable hides until needed.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Caption always visible at bottom (compact). Tap on photo opens lightbox. Action button (heart, share) always visible in top-right corner.
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Trackpad attached: hover behavior. Touch only: same as mobile.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Photo shows clean, no overlay. Hover reveals caption (slide-up from bottom) and action buttons. Cursor shows pointer.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab focuses photo, focus ring appears, caption appears (focus-visible parity with hover). Tab again to action button, etc.

performance_notes: All hover-vs-touch decisions are CSS-only — no JS detection cost. The useHoverCapable hook is provided for rare cases (e.g., showing a tooltip on hover only) but unused for layout. Pointer media queries are evaluated by the browser once and re-evaluated only when the user plugs/unplugs an input device.
accessibility_across_viewports: :focus-within parity ensures keyboard users get the same overlay reveal as mouse hover. Action buttons are 44x44pt always (h-11 w-11). aria-label on each action gives screen readers context. Photo button has explicit aria-label including the alt text. stopPropagation on action button clicks prevents accidental open.
edge_cases_handled: iPad with Magic Keyboard trackpad attached — (hover: hover) becomes true, switches to hover behavior. Detach trackpad → reverts to always-visible.
Surface Pro in tablet mode (no keyboard) — touch behavior, always-visible actions
User Tab-navigates through gallery — focus ring appears, caption reveals via focus-within parity
Mouse user accidentally taps photo with touchpad — onClick fires once (not duplicated)
iOS long-press on image — system save sheet still works because we don't preventDefault on touch events
what_breaks_naive_approach: Detecting touch via 'ontouchstart' in window returns true on Surface Pro (which also has mouse) — your gallery shows touch-mode overlays even though the user has a precision pointer. Or worse: detects 'no touch' on iPad with mouse plugged in, shows hover-only UI, then user touches the screen and nothing happens. (hover: hover) and (pointer: fine) is the correct primary signal — it asks 'CAN the user hover precisely', not 'is there a touchscreen'.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Hybrid devices (iPad + trackpad, Surface Pro) get the right experience as the user changes input mode. 2) Keyboard parity via :focus-within — keyboard users aren't second-class. 3) Touch fallback is ALWAYS-VISIBLE, not long-press — discoverable and doesn't conflict with system gestures.

title: visualViewport-aware sticky form footer that floats above the iOS soft keyboard
viewport_spectrum: iPhone SE 375x667, iPhone 14 Pro 393x852, Pixel 7 412x915, iPad in keyboard-summoned state, Android with floating keyboard, iOS landscape with keyboard taking 60%+ of screen.
scenario_context: A multi-step signup form has a sticky 'Continue' button at the bottom of the screen. On iOS, when the user focuses an input, the soft keyboard appears and the button is HIDDEN BEHIND it. On Android the layout resizes and the button is pushed up correctly. The native behaviors differ — iOS doesn't shrink the viewport, just overlays the keyboard. Need a solution that works on both.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using 100vh and assuming it represents visible space (ignores keyboard)
Listening to window.resize for keyboard events (doesn't fire on iOS)
Using position: fixed bottom: 0 (sits behind keyboard on iOS)
Setting padding-bottom in body to push content (form layout breaks)
Using interactive-widget=resizes-content meta tag without fallback (limited support)
responsive_reasoning:
1. Fundamental change: viewport visible area shrinks when keyboard appears, but window.innerHeight on iOS DOESN'T update. visualViewport.height does. This is THE only reliable signal.
2. Architecture: separate layout viewport (where content is laid out) from visual viewport (what user sees). Sticky elements should track visual viewport.
3. Input model: this issue ONLY exists on touch devices with soft keyboards. Desktop is irrelevant.
4. Performance: visualViewport.resize fires frequently during keyboard animation — use rAF throttle.
5. Accessibility: focused input must remain visible. Sticky button must not occlude content user is editing.
6. Edge cases: floating keyboard (Android can have a small floating keyboard), keyboard with suggestions strip (extra height), landscape orientation, keyboard dismissal animation.
7. Rejected: viewport-fit=cover alone (handles safe areas but not keyboard). Rejected: useEffect on window.resize (no fire on iOS).

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Soft keyboard appearance and visible viewport height
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: window.visualViewport API + resize + scroll events
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default to no-keyboard state on server. CSS env(keyboard-inset-height, 0px) provides a CSS-native fallback where supported.
detection_strategy.code_pattern: visualViewport.addEventListener('resize', () => { const kb = window.innerHeight - visualViewport.height; document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--kb', `${Math.max(0, kb)}px`); });

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS custom property updated via visualViewport JS, with env(keyboard-inset-height) progressive enhancement
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Continuous — keyboard heights vary (Apple SK, third-party, with/without suggestions)
layout_strategy.content_priority: Currently-focused input must stay visible. Sticky CTA stays above keyboard.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Form remains scrollable. Sticky elements adjust position; content reflow follows.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap input → keyboard appears → CTA stays visible (floats above) → user types → tap CTA without dismissing keyboard
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same behavior; iPad's keyboard takes ~40% of landscape screen
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: No keyboard handling needed — CTA simply sticks to bottom of viewport
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Same as mouse — physical keyboard doesn't affect viewport

performance_notes: visualViewport events throttled via rAF (single update per frame max). CSS custom property updates are GPU-friendly via transform — no layout recalc. Only one listener for the whole app (not per component). Cleanup removes property on unmount.
accessibility_across_viewports: Inputs have min-h-11 (44pt). Font-size on inputs is 16px+ to prevent iOS auto-zoom on focus. autoComplete + inputMode attributes give screen readers and password managers proper context. scrollIntoView on focus keeps the active input visible regardless of keyboard height.
edge_cases_handled: iOS with floating keyboard (iPad) — visualViewport reports correct height; inset adjusts
Android with suggestion strip — keyboard inset includes strip height
User dismisses keyboard via swipe — visualViewport fires resize, inset returns to 0
Landscape orientation with keyboard — keyboard takes 60%+ but CTA still floats above
User has form auto-fill suggestion bar — extra height included in keyboard inset
Older iOS without visualViewport support — graceful fallback to standard behavior, CTA may sit behind keyboard but is reachable via scroll
what_breaks_naive_approach: position: fixed bottom: 0 sits at the bottom of the LAYOUT viewport, which iOS does NOT shrink when the keyboard appears. The button is visually underneath the keyboard, completely inaccessible. window.innerHeight on iOS doesn't change. The ONLY reliable signal is visualViewport.height. interactive-widget=resizes-content in the viewport meta is a newer enhancement but not universally supported, so JS shim is required as the safety net.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) visualViewport API is the only correct primitive for keyboard detection — this solution uses it properly with rAF throttling. 2) Transform-based translation = GPU-accelerated, smooth even during keyboard animation. 3) interactive-widget=resizes-content in meta + JS fallback = progressive enhancement story.

title: Bottom navigation bar that respects iPhone home indicator and notch in landscape
viewport_spectrum: iPhone X through 15 Pro Max (notch + home indicator), iPhone 14/15 Pro (Dynamic Island), Android with gesture navigation pill, devices in landscape where the notch sits on left or right edge.
scenario_context: A social app's bottom navigation tab bar is positioned at the bottom of the screen. On iPhones with home indicator, the tab bar items are HIDDEN behind the home indicator. In landscape with notch on the left, the leftmost tab is hidden by the notch. Need a bar that adapts to safe areas dynamically including landscape orientation.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using padding-bottom: 34px hardcoded (only works on one device)
padding-bottom: env(safe-area-inset-bottom) without viewport-fit=cover meta (returns 0)
Ignoring landscape — safe-area-inset-left/right are equally important
Applying safe-area padding to the WRONG element (parent padding pushes everything; tab bar item padding makes only items shrink)
Forgetting min() with max() — at very small viewports the safe area can be larger than the bar itself
responsive_reasoning:
1. Fundamental change: device chrome (notch, home indicator, dynamic island, gesture pill) creates UNUSABLE areas of the screen that the OS doesn't tell the app about except through env() variables.
2. Architecture: safe areas are properties of the device, not the viewport. Treat them as constants that the layout adapts around.
3. Input model: gesture areas conflict with edge swipes. Web apps must keep tap targets out of system gesture zones (~20px from edges in iOS).
4. Performance: pure CSS, zero JS, zero runtime cost.
5. Accessibility: the navigation must remain reachable. Tap targets must remain 44pt AFTER safe-area padding is applied.
6. Edge cases: landscape with notch on left vs right (iPhone in landscape orientation determines which side), iPad split view, foldable devices with hinges.
7. Rejected: hardcoded paddings per device — too many devices. Rejected: JS detection of notch — doesn't tell you the safe area height.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Safe area insets on each side
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS env(safe-area-inset-top|right|bottom|left) variables
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: CSS-only, server-rendered HTML uses the values immediately. viewport-fit=cover meta tag is required in <head>.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS env() + max() to enforce minimum padding even when inset is 0
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Continuous — env() values are device-specific, not viewport-driven.
layout_strategy.content_priority: Tab bar items remain reachable. Visual padding wraps around device chrome.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Number of visible tab items doesn't change. Spacing around them adapts.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tab items above home indicator. In landscape, items shift right of left-side notch.
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same — iPads with home indicator (no Home button models) get the same treatment.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Bottom nav not typically present on desktop; if used, env() returns 0, falls back to baseline padding.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab key navigates to tab items; focus visible regardless of safe-area offset.

performance_notes: Pure CSS env() values — zero JS, zero observers. backdrop-filter is GPU-accelerated. supports-[backdrop-filter] feature query provides fallback opacity for older browsers that would otherwise show transparent navbar.
accessibility_across_viewports: Tab items are min-h-14 (56pt) — exceeds 44pt minimum even after considering touch area near edges. aria-current=page identifies active tab to screen readers. nav has aria-label. Focus-visible outline appears inside the tab item (outline-offset negative) so it's not clipped by parent padding. tap-highlight-transparent removes default iOS gray tap flash.
edge_cases_handled: iPhone 15 in landscape — notch on left, safe-area-inset-left ~50px pushes content right, no items hidden
iPhone SE (no notch) — env values are 0, max() falls back to 0.25rem baseline
iPad with home indicator — bottom inset ~20px, tab bar floats above
Foldable Pixel Fold in tabletop mode — env values reflect hinge position
Browser without viewport-fit=cover — env returns 0 unconditionally, max() ensures minimum padding still applied
User in PWA standalone mode — full safe area respected
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoded padding-bottom: 34px works on iPhone X but breaks on iPhone 12 (different home indicator size) and Galaxy S22 (no home indicator at all — now has too much padding). Forgetting viewport-fit=cover in the meta tag makes env() return 0 on iOS regardless. Forgetting safe-area-inset-left|right means the leftmost tab is hidden by the notch in landscape orientation.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Uses env() with max() so layout works on devices WITH and WITHOUT safe areas — single code path. 2) Handles all four insets (top/right/bottom/left) so landscape orientation works correctly. 3) viewport-fit=cover in meta is set, making env() values non-zero where applicable.

title: Video player that adapts UI placement to orientation, not just size
viewport_spectrum: Phone portrait (393x852), phone landscape (852x393), tablet portrait (820x1180), tablet landscape (1180x820), ultrawide 21:9 monitor (3440x1440), square viewport (rare embedded).
scenario_context: A video streaming app's player has controls (play, scrub, volume, fullscreen) and an info panel (title, description, related videos). In portrait, the video occupies the top third and info fills the rest. In landscape, the video should fill the screen and controls overlay; info disappears or moves to a side drawer. Pure width-based media queries treat 1024x768 (landscape iPad) the same as 768x1024 (portrait iPad) at the same width — wrong.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using (max-width: 768px) for portrait detection (wrong — width doesn't tell orientation)
Using window.orientation (deprecated)
Listening to orientationchange event (fires too late, doesn't fire on rotation lock)
Forcing landscape via screen.orientation.lock() without user gesture (browser rejects)
Treating ultrawide as 'extra landscape' (different needs entirely)
responsive_reasoning:
1. Fundamental change: in portrait, vertical space is plentiful, horizontal is scarce — stack content. In landscape, horizontal is plentiful, vertical is scarce — overlay or sidebar. Aspect ratio drives the decision, not viewport size.
2. Architecture: orientation is one CSS media query — (orientation: portrait/landscape). Combine with aspect-ratio for more nuance.
3. Input model: full-screen video on phones uses landscape orientation as a signal that user wants immersive viewing. Controls auto-hide.
4. Performance: video element resizing is GPU-accelerated. Controls overlay uses position: absolute on a parent with aspect-ratio reservation to prevent CLS.
5. Accessibility: keyboard controls work in any orientation. Closed captions remain visible in both orientations.
6. Edge cases: rotation mid-playback, picture-in-picture, fullscreen API, user has orientation lock enabled.
7. Rejected: width-based detection — fails for 1024x768 vs 768x1024 (same width, opposite orientation).

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Device/viewport orientation and aspect ratio
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: @media (orientation: landscape) + @media (aspect-ratio: ...)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default to portrait on server (more common for mobile signups). Hydration safely transitions via CSS.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS Grid with orientation-aware template + aspect-ratio container for video
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Orientation is a discrete state, but within each orientation the layout is fluid.
layout_strategy.content_priority: Portrait: video on top, info below scrollable. Landscape: video full-screen, info as side drawer (tablet) or hidden (phone).
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Landscape phone hides info pane entirely (revealed via tap → drawer). Tablet landscape shows info as side panel. Ultrawide shows full info beside video.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Portrait: tap video to show controls. Landscape: tap for controls overlay, double-tap to skip ±10s. Swipe down to exit fullscreen.
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same gestures + drag the divider to resize video/info split
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover shows controls. Click-and-drag scrubber. Spacebar play/pause. F for fullscreen.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Spacebar play/pause, arrow keys seek, M mute, F fullscreen, all controls tab-accessible

performance_notes: Aspect-ratio reservation prevents layout shift during video load. playsInline prevents iOS from auto-fullscreening (breaks custom controls). object-contain prevents stretching at any aspect ratio. Controls overlay uses GPU-accelerated opacity transitions.
accessibility_across_viewports: All buttons min h-11 w-11 (44pt). aria-label on every control. Range input has aria-label for scrubber. Spacebar/arrow keys work natively on video element. Controls remain accessible when overlay is hidden via focus (showControls becomes true on focus-within in production).
edge_cases_handled: User rotates mid-playback — CSS @media (orientation) re-evaluates, layout adapts without remount, video keeps playing
iPad split view at 320px effective width — still respects orientation queries
Ultrawide monitor 21:9 — landscape large variant gives info pane fixed 320px, video fills rest
User has orientation lock — query stays at locked orientation regardless of physical device rotation
Picture-in-picture exit — returns to inline player at current orientation
Fullscreen API on iPhone — video goes to native fullscreen player (Safari behavior)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Width-based media queries treat 1024x768 (iPad landscape) the same as 768x1024 (iPad portrait at width 1024 in some embedded contexts). The user rotates their tablet and the layout DOESN'T CHANGE because the width hasn't changed enough to cross a breakpoint. orientation media query is the only correct signal — it reflects the actual orientation.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Orientation drives layout, not just width — handles rotation correctly. 2) aspect-ratio reservation prevents layout shift across orientations. 3) Info panel placement is fundamentally different per orientation (below in portrait, beside in landscape tablet, hidden on landscape phone) — three distinct experiences, one component.

title: Tablet-specific layout for the 768-1024 dead zone — portrait iPad versus landscape iPad versus Surface
viewport_spectrum: iPad portrait 820x1180, iPad landscape 1180x820, iPad mini 744x1133, Surface Pro 9 1440x960, iPad Pro 12.9 1024x1366 portrait, Split View 320-694px effective width, Stage Manager windowed apps.
scenario_context: A project management dashboard works great on phone (single column) and desktop (sidebar + content + inspector). The tablet experience is broken: at 768px portrait it shows desktop layout cramped to fit; at 1024px landscape it shows mobile layout wasting space. Need genuine tablet-aware design that recognizes input model AND viewport constraints together.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Treating 768-1023 as 'small desktop' (cramped controls)
Treating 768-1023 as 'big mobile' (wastes space)
Single breakpoint at 768 without considering orientation
Ignoring Split View / Stage Manager which can put a 'tablet' at 320px
Assuming tablet = touch only (Surface Pro has trackpad and pen)
responsive_reasoning:
1. Fundamental change: tablets have desktop-grade screens with phone-grade input. The information density of desktop is comfortable to view but uncomfortable to interact with via touch. The compact density of mobile is uncomfortable to view but easy to touch.
2. Architecture: tablet gets its OWN layout decisions — not a desktop downgrade or mobile upgrade. Use orientation + width + pointer to identify tablet context.
3. Input model: touch primary, with optional trackpad/keyboard. Design for touch first, enhance for pointer when available.
4. Performance: tablets often have more CPU than low-end phones but slower than desktop. Bundle splitting and lazy loading still matter.
5. Accessibility: 44pt tap targets enforced. Keyboard navigation works with attached keyboards.
6. Edge cases: Stage Manager (iPadOS 16+) puts apps in arbitrary window sizes, Split View takes 320-694px effective width, Surface Pro can be touch-only or with keyboard/trackpad.
7. Rejected: 'tablet = 768-1023 always' — Stage Manager breaks this.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Combination: width range, orientation, primary pointer type
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: matchMedia composite query: (min-width: 768px) and (max-width: 1279px) and (pointer: coarse)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Use Sec-CH-UA-Mobile and viewport-width client hints if available; otherwise default to desktop layout SSR.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS Grid with three different templates: mobile (1 col), tablet (2 col), desktop (3 col)
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Hard breakpoints at 768 and 1280 because layout topology changes. Within each, fluid spacing.
layout_strategy.content_priority: Tablet portrait: list + detail (master-detail). Tablet landscape: list + detail + actions toolbar. Desktop: list + detail + inspector.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Tablet shows 2 panes (60% / 40%). Desktop shows 3 panes (20% / 55% / 25%). Mobile shows 1 pane with navigation between.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Single pane navigation: list → detail (full screen) → back button
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Two pane master-detail: tap list item updates right pane. Swipe right on detail goes back to list-only view.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Three pane simultaneous: click list item updates detail, inspector shows context-specific actions
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab through panes, arrow keys within list, Escape closes inspector

performance_notes: Each layout renders only what's needed — desktop loads inspector data, mobile doesn't. Reduces bundle and request load for smaller devices. ProjectInspector is a candidate for dynamic import based on device. No double-rendering with CSS-hidden trees.
accessibility_across_viewports: Mobile: 'Back' button preserves nav stack semantically. Tablet/desktop: list items have aria-current=true on active. Three pane desktop maintains landmark roles (nav, main, complementary). Tab order is logical in each layout — list → detail → inspector.
edge_cases_handled: iPad in Split View at 320px effective — falls into mobile layout despite physical iPad device
Stage Manager arbitrary window at 700px — matchMedia returns 'tablet-portrait' or 'mobile' based on actual width
Surface Pro detached from keyboard mid-use — pointer media query updates, but layout stays (width-based here)
iPad rotation portrait→landscape — layout adapts from 40/60 split to wider list
Touch user double-taps on selected item — debounce handled by setState (no harm)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Treating 768-1023 as 'small desktop' shows three columns at 768px with each column ~250px wide — list items truncate badly, inspector becomes unusable. Treating it as 'big mobile' wastes the entire right half of the screen on a portrait iPad. Neither extreme is right; the tablet needs its OWN two-column master-detail design.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Tablet has its own distinct layout (not a desktop downgrade or mobile upgrade). 2) Stage Manager / Split View handled correctly because matchMedia uses actual viewport width, not device type. 3) Touch-optimized list variant at tablet sizes — larger tap targets that respect tablet ergonomics.

title: Three-column master-detail-inspector with persistent resizable panes and graceful collapse
viewport_spectrum: 1920px wide desktop (full three pane), 1440px laptop (three pane compact), 1280px small laptop (two pane, inspector toggleable), 1024px tablet landscape (two pane), 768px tablet portrait (master OR detail), 375px phone (single pane with nav)
scenario_context: An email client (Gmail / Apple Mail style) has folder list, message list, and message body. Users on large monitors want all three visible AND want to drag the column dividers to customize. On tablet they want two panes. On phone, single pane with back navigation. Pane widths should persist via localStorage but adapt when viewport changes.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed width columns that don't account for content
Resize handles that don't work on touch
localStorage widths that ignore viewport (saving 600px sidebar then loading on 320px viewport)
Showing/hiding panes with display:none (loses inspector state when switching back)
ResizeObserver without rAF throttle (jittery during drag)
responsive_reasoning:
1. Fundamental change: at every viewport the user is doing the same task (read email) but the SPATIAL relationship changes. Three columns is parallel viewing; two columns is sequential with peek; one column is purely sequential.
2. Architecture: pane visibility is state. Pane widths are state. Both persisted independently per viewport class.
3. Input model: mouse drags column dividers precisely. Touch needs larger handles + may use double-tap to collapse instead.
4. Performance: resize during drag uses requestAnimationFrame + CSS Grid template update. Avoid React state during drag (use refs + direct style mutation).
5. Accessibility: dividers must be keyboard-resizable (arrow keys when focused). Role='separator' with aria-valuenow/min/max.
6. Edge cases: rotating tablet from landscape to portrait should hide the third pane, not crash. Restoring panes after viewport returns.
7. Rejected: react-resizable-panels for this — too heavy, doesn't handle viewport-class persistence cleanly. Roll a hook.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport class for which panes are visible, container size for clamping pane widths
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: matchMedia for class, ResizeObserver on root for clamping during drag
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default to detail-only on server; hydrate to full layout on client. Restore widths from cookie not localStorage for SSR.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS Grid with grid-template-columns driven by CSS custom properties
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Hard breakpoints determine pane COUNT; within each count, widths are user-resizable and fluid.
layout_strategy.content_priority: Folder list always visible if any panes shown. Detail always visible. Inspector first to collapse.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Each pane preserves its data even when hidden — no remount on viewport change.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Single pane, swipe right edge to reveal folder list, tap message to go to detail, swipe right to go back
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Two panes, drag center divider to resize, double-tap divider to reset to 40/60
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Three panes, drag dividers, hover divider shows resize cursor, double-click reset
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab to divider, arrow keys resize (5px each, 50px with Shift), Home/End to extremes

performance_notes: Drag uses pointer events + rAF — no React state thrash. Final position commits to state once. localStorage write deferred to pointerup. ResizeObserver not needed because Grid handles fluid 1fr middle column. Each pane independently scrollable (overflow-y-auto) — no nested layout cost.
accessibility_across_viewports: Resizers have role=separator with proper aria-valuenow/min/max. Keyboard resize works (arrow keys, Shift for big steps, Home/End for extremes). Touch resizers are wider (w-2 vs w-1) via pointer media query. Focus ring visible on resizer. Each pane is a landmark (aside/main).
edge_cases_handled: Restoring saved widths that exceed current viewport — clamped to min/max
Rotating tablet landscape→portrait — inspector pane vanishes, folder+detail remain
User resizes window during drag — rAF throttle handles smoothly
localStorage disabled (Safari private mode) — falls back to defaults, no crash
Keyboard-only user resizing — works via tabIndex on separator
Touch user dragging — pointer events work for both mouse and touch
what_breaks_naive_approach: Storing pane widths in localStorage without viewport class means a user who sized panes on their 4K monitor opens the app on their laptop and gets a folder list wider than the viewport. Or worse: using React state during pointermove causes 60fps re-renders of the entire pane content while dragging — janky on low-end devices.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Per-viewport-class persistence — desktop widths and tablet widths stored separately, never conflict. 2) Pointer events + rAF + direct DOM mutation during drag = 60fps even on mid-range hardware. 3) Keyboard-resizable separators meet ARIA APG pattern.

title: Priority+ data table: keep critical columns visible, overflow into expandable row on small viewports
viewport_spectrum: 320px (1 column + expand), 480px (2 cols), 768px (4 cols), 1024px (6 cols), 1440px+ (all 8 cols)
scenario_context: A SaaS billing dashboard shows invoice ID, date, customer, amount, status, payment method, due date, actions. On mobile, naive 'collapse to card' approach loses sortability and bulk selection. Need priority+ pattern: show top N columns, hide overflow into a per-row expand panel, preserve all functionality.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: overflow-x: auto on mobile (horizontal scroll loses sticky context)
Cards on mobile that drop columns silently (data loss)
Hiding columns by index without priority awareness
Forgetting sort/filter still need to work on hidden cols
responsive_reasoning:
1. The table is the same data graph at every viewport — only what's VISIBLE changes. Hidden columns remain in the DOM (or expand panel) so sort/filter/bulk still function.
2. Architecture: each column declares a priority (1=critical, 5=nice-to-have). Visible-column count is a function of viewport width. Hidden columns auto-collapse into expand panel.
3. Touch: row expand via tap on row OR explicit chevron (44pt). Bulk checkbox is min 44pt with extra hit area.
4. Performance: virtualize long lists at any viewport. Sticky header works at all sizes.
5. Accessibility: hidden columns get aria-rowindex/colindex preserved. Screen reader navigation works.
6. Edge cases: very long customer names truncate with title attribute; numeric columns right-aligned; status badges scale.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Container width to determine visible column count
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: Container queries on the table wrapper
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Render all columns server-side; CSS hides via container queries.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS table with container queries hiding low-priority columns; expand panel below row shows hidden columns as definition list
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Container query breakpoints aligned with column priority counts
layout_strategy.content_priority: Priority 1 (ID, amount): always visible. Priority 2-3: visible at >=768. Priority 4-5: visible at >=1024.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Density preserved via expand panel — no data is lost, only relocated

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap row to expand and see hidden columns. Bulk checkbox at row left (44pt). Sort via dropdown above table.
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: More columns visible, expand still works for remaining hidden ones
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: All columns visible at 1440+, expand panel disabled (no hidden cols)
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab through cells, Enter expands row, Space toggles checkbox

performance_notes: Container queries are CSS-native — no JS detection. Sticky thead works at all sizes. tabular-nums for aligned numerics. truncate + title preserves data on hover. Virtualize wrapper (not shown) for >100 rows.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real <table> with <th scope='col'> — screen readers announce headers when navigating cells. aria-expanded on expand button. Checkbox has aria-label. Sort buttons are real buttons. Hidden columns DO remain in the DOM via expand panel (not display: none on data) — screen readers can reach all data.
edge_cases_handled: Container narrower than 320px (extreme split-view) — only ID + amount + expand visible
Row with 12 hidden columns expanded — dl renders correctly with grid layout
Sort on hidden column — works because all data in DOM, sort triggers re-render
Bulk select 1000 rows — checkbox state in Set, O(1) operations
Long customer name 'Internationale Handelsgesellschaft mbH' — truncates with title tooltip
what_breaks_naive_approach: Card-on-mobile loses bulk selection (no checkbox column on cards), loses sort (no column headers), and often loses data (cards typically show 3 fields, table has 8). Horizontal scroll on small screens loses sticky context — user scrolls right to see status, can't see which customer it belongs to. Priority+ keeps the table model intact.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same table component, every viewport — no fork to cards. 2) Container queries on table wrapper (not viewport) — works inside narrow sidebars too. 3) Hidden data is preserved in expand panel — sort/filter/bulk-select all still function across all columns.

title: Date input that uses native picker on mobile, segmented fields on desktop, and survives autofill
viewport_spectrum: 375px iOS (date wheel), 412px Android (calendar dialog), 768px tablet (native or fallback), 1280px+ desktop (segmented MM/DD/YYYY with inline calendar)
scenario_context: A booking form needs a date-of-birth field. Native <input type='date'> is perfect on mobile (OS picker) but ugly and limited on desktop. Most teams pick one approach. We want both: native on touch devices, custom segmented input on desktop with full keyboard navigation.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: JS-only date picker on mobile (worse than native)
Custom UI on desktop that doesn't support keyboard typing of date
Forgetting locale-aware date formatting (MM/DD vs DD/MM)
Not handling autofill correctly (Chrome fills 'birthday' into custom UI)
Forms that need a click on a calendar icon (breaks keyboard flow)
responsive_reasoning:
1. Native input type=date is genuinely better on mobile (OS-tuned UX). On desktop it's worse than a custom component (no keyboard fluency, locked format). Detect pointer type to pick.
2. Hidden native input for autofill participation — desktop custom UI syncs to it.
3. Touch: thumb-friendly OS picker. Desktop: type 12251990 and it formats as 12/25/1990.
4. Accessibility: aria-label on each segment, keyboard arrows to change, screen reader announces full date on commit.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Primary pointer type (coarse=touch)
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: @media (pointer: coarse) CSS + matchMedia for JS-level swap
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Render native input as default; desktop swap happens client-side after hydration to avoid mismatch.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Conditional component swap based on pointer type
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Categorical — touch vs precise pointer
layout_strategy.content_priority: Both modes capture same date data; rendering differs
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Mobile: single field that opens OS picker. Desktop: 3 segments visible + calendar trigger button

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap field → OS date picker (wheel on iOS, calendar on Android)
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same as mobile if touch primary; desktop UI if trackpad/keyboard primary
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Click segment to focus, type digits to fill, auto-advance to next segment, calendar icon opens popup
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Arrow keys change segment values, /, , -, or numbers advance to next segment, Enter opens calendar

performance_notes: Native input on mobile is zero-JS for the picker UI. Desktop segments are 3 lightweight inputs. No date library required for basic validation. Hidden type=date enables autofill without UI conflict.
accessibility_across_viewports: Mobile uses OS-native picker — perfectly accessible by OS standards. Desktop: each segment has aria-label (Month/Day/Year). Number-only inputMode triggers numeric keyboard on hybrid devices. Auto-advance respects max length; user can also type / or - to advance. Hidden native input enables password-manager-style autofill.
edge_cases_handled: Chrome autofills 'birthday' field — hidden input fires onChange, syncs segments
User pastes '12/25/1990' into month field — regex extracts digits, distributes across segments
iPad with trackpad — pointer:coarse may be true or false depending on input; both UIs work
Locale DD/MM users — order can be swapped via locale prop (not shown)
Year typed as 90 — could expand to 1990 with sensible heuristic; currently requires 4 digits
what_breaks_naive_approach: JS-only date picker on iOS is universally worse than the native wheel — slower, less familiar, harder to reach with thumb. Custom picker on desktop without keyboard support (only mouse on calendar) breaks power users. Forgetting autocomplete='bday' loses autofill entirely.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Best UX for each input modality — native where OS shines, custom where keyboard matters. 2) Hidden native input ensures autofill works regardless of UI shown. 3) Auto-advance segments with multiple separator chars (/, -, space) makes typing as fast as the best desktop date pickers.

title: Art-directed responsive images with picture element, srcset, sizes, and aspect-ratio reservation
viewport_spectrum: 320px portrait (square crop), 768px portrait (4:3), 1024px landscape (16:9), 1920px landscape (21:9 cinematic), 4K (high DPR concerns)
scenario_context: A travel website hero image needs different CROPS at different viewports — phone shows tight portrait crop of subject, desktop shows wide landscape with environment. Beyond resolution selection, this is art direction. Plus we need lazy loading, modern formats (AVIF/WebP), and zero CLS.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single at 4000px wide (mobile downloads 2MB image)
Using only srcset without sizes (browser picks wrong density)
object-fit: cover without aspect-ratio (CLS until image loads)
Same composition cropped differently (subject gets cut off)
Lazy loading without an explicit width/height (layout shift on load)
responsive_reasoning:
1. Art direction requires SOURCE swap, not just size swap. picture+source is the only correct tool.
2. Within each source, srcset+sizes lets the browser pick optimal resolution for DPR/viewport.
3. aspect-ratio CSS reserves space before image loads — zero CLS.
4. AVIF for modern, WebP for fallback, JPEG ultimate fallback via type negotiation.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport size (for art direction), DPR (for resolution)
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: picture element media attribute + srcset 'w' descriptors + sizes
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure HTML — server renders, browser picks before any JS runs

layout_strategy.primary_technique: picture element + aspect-ratio CSS + container queries for crop selection
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Discrete breakpoints for crops, fluid sizing within each
layout_strategy.content_priority: Subject always visible. Composition adapts to viewport orientation.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Mobile: tight subject focus. Desktop: subject + environment context

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Hero is decorative; pinch to zoom not needed; tap to dismiss/CTA
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover may reveal caption
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Focus-visible on CTA inside hero

performance_notes: Browser parses picture BEFORE running any JS — picks optimal source/resolution during preload scan. AVIF is ~50% smaller than JPEG for equivalent quality. fetchpriority=high tells browser to prioritize hero. Lazy loading is intentionally NOT used here (above the fold), but used for non-hero images. aspect-ratio CSS reserves space — zero CLS.
accessibility_across_viewports: alt text describes the scene (not 'image of'). Decorative variants would use alt=''. Hero text is real HTML inside <picture>'s parent — screen readers read content normally, regardless of which crop was selected.
edge_cases_handled: User on 4K monitor with 1x DPR — picks ultrawide-2560w via srcset
User on iPhone 14 Pro at 3x DPR — picks portrait-1080w (360 viewport × 3 DPR = ~1080)
Browser without AVIF — falls through type negotiation to WebP, then JPEG
Slow 3G — fallback img with smaller intrinsic size loads while modern formats wait
User in iPad split-view 320px — uses portrait sources, smallest resolution
Print stylesheet — picture works in print, picks the JPEG fallback
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single at desktop resolution forces 320px mobile users to download a 1920px image — 2MB+ on a phone, kills LCP. srcset without sizes makes browser assume 100vw, downloads way too much. Same crop at all sizes means a wide hero subject becomes tiny when cropped to portrait — defeats the purpose of art direction.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Three distinct crops (portrait/landscape/ultrawide) — true art direction, not just resizing. 2) Modern format negotiation via type attribute — AVIF where supported, no JS detection. 3) aspect-ratio per breakpoint reserves correct space at every viewport — zero CLS regardless of which source loads.

title: Adaptive primary nav: mega menu desktop, drawer tablet, bottom nav mobile, with state preserved across transitions
viewport_spectrum: 1440px+ (mega menu hover), 1024-1439px (drawer with top trigger), 768-1023px (drawer), <768px (bottom nav 4-5 items)
scenario_context: An e-commerce site nav has 8 top-level categories, each with 10-30 sub-items. Mega menu on desktop, drawer on tablet, bottom tab bar on mobile. When the user resizes the window mid-session, the open menu state must transfer correctly (or close gracefully).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Always-mounted three nav components with CSS display:none (a11y nightmare, 3x DOM cost)
Closing all menus on resize (loses user context)
Hamburger menu on desktop (industry consensus: stop)
Bottom nav with 7 items (touch targets too small)
Drawer that doesn't trap focus when open
responsive_reasoning: 1. Three different INTERACTION MODELS: hover-reveal (desktop), slide-in (tablet), persistent-visible (mobile). Not just sizing differences.\n2. Architecture: shared nav data, three render components, one router. Active state tracked once.\n3. Mobile bottom nav surfaces top 4 categories; the 5th 'More' opens a sheet with the rest. Desktop mega menu shows all 8.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport class + first-interaction (don't auto-open menu on resize)
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: useBreakpoint hook + state machine for menu visibility
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Render mobile bottom nav skeleton by default; swap to desktop after hydration

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Adaptive component swap with shared data layer
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Categorical at nav boundaries (different components)
layout_strategy.content_priority: Top 4 categories prioritized for mobile bottom nav. Rest in 'More' sheet.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Desktop: 8 categories x ~20 subitems all visible on hover. Mobile: 4 visible + sheet for rest

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Bottom nav 5 items, tap 'More' opens bottom sheet with all 8 categories
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Hamburger button (acceptable here because tablet drawer is genuinely useful), drawer slides from left
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover top-level → mega menu opens below, mouse-out closes after 300ms grace
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab through top-level, Enter opens mega menu, Escape closes, arrows within submenu

performance_notes: Only one nav component mounts at a time — no triple-render. Mega menu only renders submenus on hover (lazy). Drawer locks body scroll while open. Bottom nav is always-visible (no transitions on scroll).
accessibility_across_viewports: Mega menu: aria-expanded, aria-haspopup, hover delay prevents premature close. Drawer: role=dialog aria-modal, Escape closes, focus trap inside drawer. Bottom nav: aria-current=page. All keyboard accessible.
edge_cases_handled: User resizes from desktop to mobile with mega menu open — component swaps, menu state reset (acceptable)
Touch user accidentally hovers via stylus — touch-action handles, no stuck menus
Keyboard user Tab through mega menu, Escape closes and returns focus to trigger
Drawer open during rotation — overlay still covers, no scroll bleed
User has prefers-reduced-motion — drawer slides without animation
what_breaks_naive_approach: Rendering all 3 navs with CSS display:none triples DOM, confuses screen readers (which read all 3 nav landmarks), and means hidden mega menu still attempts hover detection. Single hamburger menu on desktop hides the site's category structure — measurably worse engagement vs always-visible nav.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Three genuinely different INTERACTION MODELS (hover/slide/persistent), not just resized versions. 2) Single nav data source — adding a category updates all three layouts. 3) Bottom nav 'More' overflow pattern handles arbitrary category counts gracefully.

title: Modal that becomes bottom sheet on mobile with proper drag-to-dismiss and focus management
viewport_spectrum: 320-767px (bottom sheet), 768-1023px (centered dialog with backdrop), 1024+ (small centered dialog or side panel)
scenario_context: A confirmation/edit modal needs to feel native on every device. iOS users expect a bottom sheet that drag-to-dismisses. Desktop users expect a centered dialog. The same React component should produce both based on viewport, with shared focus management and Escape-to-close.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Centered modal on mobile (cut off, hard to dismiss)
Bottom sheet without backdrop (no escape signal)
Drag-to-dismiss that conflicts with scroll inside sheet
Modal without focus trap (Tab escapes to background)
Returning focus to body instead of trigger on close
responsive_reasoning: 1. Bottom sheet is genuinely different UX from centered modal — drag affordance, scroll behavior, dismissal gesture. Not styling.\n2. Sheet anchored to bottom respects thumb reach. Modal centered respects desktop visual hierarchy.\n3. Both must trap focus, restore on close, lock body scroll, dismiss on Escape/backdrop/swipe.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport class for layout choice
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: useBreakpoint
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Modal is portal-rendered, usually closed by default — no SSR concern

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Portal + conditional component with shared focus/scroll lock
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Categorical at 768px (sheet vs dialog)
layout_strategy.content_priority: Same content; chrome and dismissal model differ
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Sheet may have larger touch targets and bigger title

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Sheet slides up, drag down to dismiss, tap backdrop to dismiss, primary action at bottom (thumb zone)
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Centered dialog with backdrop, click outside or X to close
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Centered dialog, Escape closes, click X or backdrop
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab traps inside, Shift+Tab wraps, Escape returns focus to trigger

performance_notes: Portal renders outside React tree — no z-index/stacking-context conflicts. Body scroll locked via overflow:hidden (with iOS-specific scroll position preservation if needed). Drag uses pointer events + direct style mutation, no React re-renders during drag.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=dialog + aria-modal=true. aria-labelledby points to title. Focus trap with Tab/Shift+Tab wrapping. Escape closes. Focus returns to trigger on close. Close button is min 44pt. Drag handle is decorative (aria-hidden) — sheet can still be dismissed via Escape/backdrop/X for non-touch users.
edge_cases_handled: User drags sheet partially then releases — snaps back to fully open
User opens dialog with keyboard then resizes window mid-open — re-renders to new variant, focus stays trapped
Sheet content longer than viewport — internal scroll, drag still works on header only (touch-none on grab area)
iOS Safari with keyboard open — visualViewport keeps sheet positioned correctly above keyboard
User has prefers-reduced-motion — no slide-up animation (handled in real CSS file)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Centered modal on a 375px viewport is uncomfortable (cropped, hard-to-reach close button). Bottom sheet without focus trap lets keyboard users Tab into background content while sheet is open — a screen-reader nightmare. Drag-to-dismiss without distinguishing handle from content makes scrolling inside sheet accidentally close it.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Genuinely different UX per viewport (sheet vs centered), same component, same a11y. 2) Drag-to-dismiss isolated to handle area — doesn't conflict with internal scroll. 3) Focus restoration to trigger element on close — critical for keyboard users.

title: Ultrawide monitor (3440x1440) layout that doesn't stretch content into unreadable lines
viewport_spectrum: 3440x1440 ultrawide, 5120x1440 super-ultrawide, 3840x2160 4K, 2560x1080 ultrawide 21:9
scenario_context: A reading-focused app (news, docs, blog) on a 3440x1440 ultrawide shows lines that are 200+ characters wide if naively containered to 100%. Eyes fatigue. Need to constrain primary content while productively using the extra horizontal space (sidebar, table of contents, related, ambient information).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: max-width: 1200px and calling it done (wastes 2200px of screen)
Letting prose flow to full width (450+ character lines, unreadable)
Three-column desktop layout that becomes too narrow per column on 1280px laptop
Center container with empty space on sides (visually empty)
responsive_reasoning: 1. On ultrawide, the question isn't 'how big' but 'what extra content can fill the space productively without distracting'. Reading width is fixed by typography (65ch); peripheral space gets ambient nav/TOC/related.\n2. Use clamp(min, ideal, max) to scale up gracefully without runaway. Cap container at sensible max.\n3. Multi-column layouts on ultrawide give users glanceable peripheral info (table of contents, comments, ambient).

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Ultrawide via width + aspect-ratio
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: @media (min-aspect-ratio: 21/9) or (min-width: 2400px)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: CSS-only, no SSR concern

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS Grid with ultrawide-specific template adding extra columns for ambient content
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Fluid up to 1440px, ultrawide breakpoint adds columns, capped beyond
layout_strategy.content_priority: Article = primary. TOC = secondary. Related/ambient = tertiary, only on ultrawide.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Ultrawide shows MORE content (TOC, related articles) but not BIGGER content

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: N/A — this is ultrawide-specific
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Single column, comfortable max-width
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Two-column (article + TOC) at 1440-2400px, three-column (+ ambient) at 2400+
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab order: nav → TOC → article → ambient (logical reading order)

performance_notes: Pure CSS layout — no JS. Sticky positioning on TOC/ambient is GPU-accelerated. minmax(0, 75ch) on middle column prevents content from forcing column wider than ch limit. Grid layout is single layout pass even on 4K.
accessibility_across_viewports: Article is real <article>. TOC is nav with aria-label. Ambient is aside (complementary landmark). Skip-to-content link still works. Sticky elements don't trap focus.
edge_cases_handled: 32:9 super-ultrawide (5120px) — capped at 2200px, generous margins prevent center stretch
User has multi-column display setup — each window gets its own layout
Ultrawide in vertical orientation (rare but exists, like portrait 4K) — falls to two-column, prose stays readable
Browser at 200% zoom on ultrawide — effectively 1720px viewport, falls to two-column gracefully
Foldable spanned across hinge — narrow each side, falls to single column
what_breaks_naive_approach: max-width: 1200px centered means a 3440px monitor has 1100px of empty gray on each side — looks broken. No max-width means line length is 250+ chars at 3440px — physically painful to read. Both extremes wrong. Constrain prose to 75ch, fill remaining space with ambient navigation.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Prose width stays optimal (~75ch) regardless of viewport — typography-first design. 2) Extra horizontal space gets productive content (TOC, ambient), not empty padding. 3) Multiple breakpoints (2400, 3200) reveal more columns progressively rather than one binary jump.

title: useReducedMotion + useColorScheme + useContrast composed user preference hook
viewport_spectrum: Any device — user preferences are device-agnostic but vary widely
scenario_context: An animated dashboard has page transitions, hover micro-interactions, and parallax. Users with vestibular disorders need animations off (prefers-reduced-motion). Users in dark theaters need dark mode (prefers-color-scheme). Users with low vision need high contrast (prefers-contrast). All three must be respected simultaneously.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Reading preferences in render (hydration mismatch)
