Using legacy addListener instead of addEventListener
Ignoring prefers-reduced-motion on parallax (vestibular hostile)
Theme toggle that overrides OS preference without saving user choice
Using only one preference (color scheme) and ignoring motion/contrast
responsive_reasoning: 1. User preferences are first-class viewport context — not all 'viewport' is about size. Motion, color, contrast, transparency, data — all device queries.\n2. Architecture: composable hooks per preference, single Provider for app-wide consumption.\n3. Allow user override on top of OS preference (toggle UI), persisted to localStorage.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Motion, color scheme, contrast, transparency preferences
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: matchMedia for prefers-reduced-motion, prefers-color-scheme, prefers-contrast, prefers-reduced-transparency
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default to no-preference (false / 'light') server-side; client useSyncExternalStore reads actual on mount

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Preference values flow via context; CSS reads prefers-* media queries as primary, JS reads for animation gating
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Boolean states, not fluid
layout_strategy.content_priority: Same content always; presentation/motion adapts
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: High-contrast may add explicit borders that low-contrast omits

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Toggle preferences via settings sheet
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Settings dropdown shows OS preference + override
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Toggles are fully keyboard-accessible

performance_notes: useSyncExternalStore avoids re-render storms. matchMedia listeners fire only on actual preference change (rare). CSS-based reduce-motion is the primary gate; JS hook only needed for non-CSS animations (Framer Motion, canvas).
accessibility_across_viewports: Respecting prefers-reduced-motion prevents vestibular triggers. prefers-color-scheme honors OS theme by default with user override. prefers-contrast strengthens borders/outlines. prefers-reduced-data can skip large images/videos. All four together = comprehensive accessibility.
edge_cases_handled: User toggles OS dark mode while app open — useSyncExternalStore fires, class updates immediately
User sets app to 'light' on dark OS — explicit override persists, ignores OS dark
User has reduced-motion ON and uses Framer Motion — useReducedMotion guards animate prop
First-paint flash of wrong theme — inline script in <head> sets class before React hydrates (not shown but standard)
Screen reader user with high-contrast forced colors mode — CSS forced-colors media query (related to prefers-contrast) adapts
what_breaks_naive_approach: Always-on parallax causes nausea in vestibular-sensitive users. Hardcoded dark theme without prefers-color-scheme respect ignores user OS setting — they're back to manually toggling on every site. Animation timings that don't gate on prefers-reduced-motion ignore an accessibility hard requirement.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Treats user preferences as first-class viewport context. 2) OS preference + user override + persistence — full control model. 3) CSS-primary, JS-secondary — animations gate via CSS by default, JS only for fine-grained motion library control.

title: Nested container queries: dashboard widgets that adapt to their parent grid cell, not the viewport
viewport_spectrum: Widget rendered in cells of varying size: 200x200, 400x300, 600x400, 800x600, 1200x800 — independently of viewport
scenario_context: A customizable dashboard where users drag widgets into a grid. Each widget might be 1x1, 2x1, 2x2, or 4x2 cells. The widget should adapt its display based on its CELL SIZE, not the viewport. A weather widget at 1x1 shows just temperature; at 4x2 shows forecast + map. Two of the same widget on the same dashboard at different sizes should render differently.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Viewport media queries (irrelevant to widget cell size)
ResizeObserver + React state for every widget (re-render storm)
Prop-drilling 'size' enum from grid to widget
Container query without container-type set (no-op)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Cell size is the meaningful axis, not viewport. Two same widgets at different cell sizes on same screen are different visual identities.\n2. Pure CSS via container queries — no JS, no re-renders.\n3. Each widget defines its OWN breakpoints based on what it displays.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Widget cell size
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS @container queries on named widget container
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure CSS, no SSR issue

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Named container queries with container-type: size (allows height queries too)
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Container breakpoints at widget-meaningful sizes
layout_strategy.content_priority: Each widget has tiered content: essential, useful, nice-to-have
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: 1x1: just data point. 2x1: data + trend. 2x2: + chart. 4x2: + detail/legend

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: All widgets touch-friendly internally
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover may reveal additional widget actions
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Each widget tabbable as a unit, internal controls accessible

performance_notes: Pure CSS container queries — zero JS, zero observers. cqi units scale temperature font size based on widget width. Adding/removing widgets doesn't require viewport recalculation, just one layout pass.
accessibility_across_viewports: Each widget is a region with accessible heading. Hidden content uses display:none — not perceived by screen readers (which is correct — at small sizes, the extra data isn't part of the widget identity). Tab order respects DOM order.
edge_cases_handled: Same widget at 1x1 and 4x2 on same page — different content rendered, single component
User resizes widget by dragging corner — content adapts in real-time, no React re-render
Widget in a column on portrait phone where column is 320px wide — automatically shows 2x1 layout
Widget intentionally placed in narrow sidebar — shows 1x1 even on desktop
User has reduced-motion — no transitions on content reveal
what_breaks_naive_approach: Viewport media queries would force ALL weather widgets to look the same regardless of cell size — impossible to have 1x1 and 2x2 versions of the same widget on the same page. Prop-based size enum forces parent to know widget internals — breaks composability. ResizeObserver + state causes re-renders on every drag pixel.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same component, multiple instances, different sizes, different content — pure CSS. 2) cqi units allow typography to scale with container, not viewport. 3) Container-type: size allows querying both width AND height for richer adaptation.

title: Pair of headings that maintain proportional spacing as both scale fluidly
viewport_spectrum: 320px to 3840px continuous
scenario_context: A hero with h1 'Welcome' and subtitle 'to our platform'. As viewport scales, both should grow proportionally — but the GAP between them should also scale, and they should maintain a precise visual relationship (subtitle ~40% of h1 size). With Tailwind responsive prefixes, they grow in different steps and the proportions break.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hardcoded gaps between headings
Different breakpoint counts for h1 vs subtitle
Tailwind text-4xl md:text-6xl lg:text-8xl on h1, text-lg md:text-xl on subtitle — proportions drift between breakpoints
responsive_reasoning: 1. Typographic relationships are PROPORTIONAL, not absolute. If h1 is at min and grows by 2x to max, subtitle should also grow by 2x to maintain ratio.\n2. Use the same clamp() multiplier for both — single source of truth.\n3. Gap between heading and subtitle scales as a fraction of heading size (em-based)

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport width via clamp middle term
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS clamp() with shared vw factor
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure CSS

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Single fluid size token, derived sizes via calc()
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Fully fluid, no breakpoints
layout_strategy.content_priority: Heading + subtitle, both always visible, proportions maintained
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Always same content; size adapts

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: N/A (typography)
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: N/A
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: N/A
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: N/A

performance_notes: Pure CSS, single clamp() evaluation, derived sizes are simple multiplications. No JS.
accessibility_across_viewports: Heading still has correct semantic level. line-height tighter on h1 (1.05) appropriate for display type, looser on body (1.4). maxWidth: 40ch keeps subtitle readable even when font is large.
edge_cases_handled: Very long title that wraps — line-height 1.05 keeps wrapped lines from colliding
User at 200% zoom — entire system scales because everything is rem/em-based
Very short title 'Hi' — left-aligned doesn't look awkward (no forced center)
RTL layout — works without changes (items-start respects direction)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tailwind responsive prefixes create steps. At 767px h1 is 2.25rem, at 768px it's 3.75rem — that's a 67% jump. Subtitle goes from 1.125rem to 1.25rem — only 11% jump. Proportions are NOT maintained across the breakpoint. clamp() with shared multiplier maintains proportions exactly.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Single source of truth for size — subtitle and gap derived from h1. 2) Fully fluid — no visible jumps. 3) Visual proportions exact at every viewport — the relationship between heading and subtitle is constant.

title: Drag-and-drop list that works with mouse, touch, AND keyboard with appropriate feedback per modality
viewport_spectrum: Mobile touch (375px), tablet touch (768px), desktop mouse (1280px+), assistive keyboard at any size
scenario_context: A task list where users reorder items. Mouse users grab and drag. Touch users long-press then drag (with haptic). Keyboard users press a 'reorder' button and use arrow keys. All three must produce the same data change and feel natural per modality.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: HTML5 drag-and-drop on mobile (doesn't work)
Touch drag without long-press delay (conflicts with scroll)
Mouse drag without grab cursor / preview
No keyboard alternative (inaccessible)
Drag library that doesn't support all three
responsive_reasoning: 1. Drag is the same DATA operation across modalities, but the INTERACTION model differs profoundly. Mouse: direct grab. Touch: hold to activate, then drag (preserves scroll). Keyboard: explicit reorder mode.\n2. dnd-kit handles all three; here we'll show conceptual code with raw events.\n3. Feedback differs per modality: cursor change (mouse), haptic (touch), focus ring + live region (keyboard).

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Active input modality
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: PointerEvent.pointerType per event + keyboard mode toggle
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default static list on server; drag enabled client-side

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Pointer events for mouse/touch unification, separate keyboard mode for accessibility
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Categorical based on input type
layout_strategy.content_priority: List remains readable; drag handle visible on hover (mouse) or always (touch)
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Touch shows drag handles by default; mouse reveals on hover

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Long-press item (300ms) to activate drag, haptic on activation, drag to position, release to drop
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same as mobile
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover shows handle, grab handle (cursor: grab → grabbing), drag preview follows cursor
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Focus item, press Space to enter reorder mode, arrow keys to move, Space again to confirm, Escape cancels

performance_notes: Pointer events unify mouse/touch — single code path. touch-none on handle prevents scroll conflict. Real implementation uses dnd-kit which provides virtualization for long lists.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-live region announces drag state changes. aria-grabbed on handle (older spec, supplemented by polite announcements). Keyboard alternative is full-featured. Handle is real button with aria-label. 44pt minimum on handle.
edge_cases_handled: Touch user starts long-press, lifts finger before 300ms — drag not initiated, scroll works
Touch user long-press then scrolls naturally — drag activates, list scrolls along during drag
Keyboard user mid-reorder when item filtered out by another action — gracefully cancels
Mouse user starts drag then alt-tabs away — pointerup outside cancels cleanly
Screen reader user — announcements describe each move
what_breaks_naive_approach: HTML5 drag-and-drop doesn't fire on mobile Safari at all — touch users can't reorder. No keyboard alternative locks out keyboard-only users. No long-press delay on touch makes scrolling impossible (every touch initiates drag).
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same data operation, three native interaction models — mouse direct drag, touch long-press, keyboard explicit mode. 2) Haptic feedback on touch activation completes the touch UX. 3) aria-live announcements make screen reader experience first-class.

title: Chat input that floats above iOS keyboard with composer height adapting to message length
viewport_spectrum: iPhone 12-15 (390x844 portrait), Android Pixel (412x915), iPad portrait (820x1180), iPad landscape (1180x820) with keyboard summoned
scenario_context: A messaging app's compose bar sits at the bottom. When user taps to type, iOS keyboard appears. The compose bar must float above the keyboard, AND the textarea must grow as the user types multi-line messages (up to a cap, then scroll inside). The message list above must scroll to bottom when keyboard appears so latest messages stay visible.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed-height textarea (cramped multi-line)
Auto-grow that doesn't cap (textarea consumes whole screen)
Scrolling list to bottom via scrollTop on every keystroke (jank)
Resize composer on window.resize (doesn't fire on iOS)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Two simultaneous responsive concerns: keyboard inset (vertical viewport change) and composer auto-grow (content-driven height). Both meet at the same bottom edge.\n2. Use visualViewport for keyboard. Use scrollHeight measurement for textarea height with cap.\n3. When keyboard appears, jump (not animate) list to bottom — animation during keyboard slide causes nausea.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Keyboard inset, textarea content height
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: visualViewport API + textarea scrollHeight on input
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Render static composer; auto-grow activates on first input

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Sticky composer with translate-y for keyboard, auto-grow textarea with max cap and internal scroll
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Continuous — keyboard height varies by device
layout_strategy.content_priority: Latest messages always visible. Composer always above keyboard.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Composer grows from 1 line to N lines based on content, capped

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap input → keyboard slides up → composer rises with it → type → composer grows → send button reachable
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same plus floating keyboard option
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: No keyboard handling; Enter sends, Shift+Enter newline
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Same as mouse — physical keyboard doesn't trigger viewport changes

performance_notes: Auto-grow uses scrollHeight measurement on each keystroke — single layout pass per character, acceptable. Cap at 160px prevents runaway. visualViewport resize handler uses rAF to coalesce with browser paint. Transform-based translate is GPU-accelerated.
accessibility_across_viewports: Textarea has aria-label. Send button has aria-label and disabled state. Enter sends on desktop (no preventDefault on touch — newline behavior is preserved for touch users who can't easily Shift+Enter). 44pt send button. text-base (16px) prevents iOS auto-zoom.
edge_cases_handled: Multi-line paste exceeding cap — textarea scrolls internally, send button reachable
User dismisses keyboard mid-type — visualViewport fires, composer slides down, list rescrolls
iOS rotation with keyboard open — visualViewport reports new height, composer adapts
User has external Bluetooth keyboard — no visualViewport change, composer doesn't move, Enter sends
Very rapid typing — autoGrow runs once per state change, no jank
what_breaks_naive_approach: position:fixed bottom:0 puts composer behind keyboard on iOS. Fixed-height textarea forces single line UX or huge always-tall input. Scrolling list to bottom on every keystroke makes typing janky.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Three simultaneous adaptive concerns handled cleanly: keyboard inset, composer auto-grow, list scroll. 2) Enter-to-send only on precise pointer devices — touch users get newline (matches native chat apps). 3) Transform + sticky combo allows composer to slide above keyboard without breaking flow layout.

title: PWA splash screen and full-bleed hero respecting all four safe areas and notch in landscape
viewport_spectrum: iPhone with notch (12-15), iPhone with Dynamic Island (14 Pro+), iPad with home indicator, Android with gesture pill, devices in landscape orientation where notch is on left or right
scenario_context: A travel app PWA installed to home screen displays a full-bleed hero image when launching. The image should extend edge-to-edge (under status bar) but text overlays must avoid safe areas. In landscape on iPhone, the notch is on one side and the home indicator is opposite — text needs to inset from both.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: padding edges only (background doesn't extend to edges)
Full bleed without text inset (text under notch)
Hardcoded edge insets per device
Missing viewport-fit=cover (env() returns 0)
Same inset for portrait and landscape (landscape needs side insets)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Full-bleed means background goes EDGE-TO-EDGE. But interactive/readable content must stay within SAFE area. These are different concerns.\n2. Background: no padding, no env(). Foreground content: padding using env() on all four sides.\n3. Landscape orientation changes which side has the notch — env(safe-area-inset-left) handles this without orientation detection.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Safe area insets per side
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: env(safe-area-inset-top/right/bottom/left)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure CSS

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Layered: background full-bleed, foreground inset via env() on all four sides
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Continuous — env() values are device-specific
layout_strategy.content_priority: Hero image visible everywhere; text/CTA in safe zone
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Landscape may show same content but with adjusted horizontal padding

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Hero touchable for CTA; tap zones inside safe area
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: No safe areas on most desktops; env returns 0
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: CTA focusable via keyboard

performance_notes: Pure CSS env(). Image with priority for fast LCP. Single layer for gradient overlay. No JS.
accessibility_across_viewports: Decorative image overlay uses aria-hidden. CTA button is min-h-12 (48pt). Focus ring visible. Text contrast over gradient ensures 4.5:1 ratio.
edge_cases_handled: iPhone in landscape with notch on left — left padding from env() shifts text right of notch
iPhone in landscape with notch on right (rotated other way) — right padding from env() shifts text left of indicator zone
iPad without home indicator — env values are 0, max() ensures min 1.25rem padding
PWA standalone vs Safari browser — env values may differ; both handled identically
Android with rounded display corners but no gesture pill — minimal env values, standard padding
what_breaks_naive_approach: viewport-fit=cover MUST be in the meta tag — without it, env() values return 0 on iOS regardless of physical device. Forgetting safe-area-inset-left/right means landscape on iPhone has text running under the notch. Same fixed padding everywhere wastes vertical space on devices without notches.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Background and foreground decoupled — image fills edges, content stays safe. 2) All four insets handled — works in any rotation. 3) max() with env() guarantees breathing room even when env returns 0.

title: Photo gallery grid that switches between 3-col and 5-col based on viewport aspect ratio not just width
viewport_spectrum: Tall phone portrait (375x812), wide phone landscape (812x375), iPad portrait (820x1180), iPad landscape (1180x820), ultrawide (2560x1080)
scenario_context: A photo gallery uses CSS Grid auto-fit. At 812px landscape phone, naive width-based logic gives 3 columns (like tablet portrait at 768px). But landscape phone has very limited vertical space — 5 small columns shows more photos at once and is closer to the iOS Photos app behavior. Aspect ratio matters more than absolute width.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: grid-cols-3 at 768+ (treats landscape phone like portrait tablet)
Fixed column counts independent of orientation
Ignoring user's preference for density
responsive_reasoning: 1. Aspect ratio > absolute width for grid density decisions. Wider+shorter = more columns. Taller = fewer columns to keep thumbnails reasonable size.\n2. Combine media queries: width AND aspect-ratio.\n3. Auto-fit with minmax provides natural fallback.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport width + aspect ratio
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: @media combining (min-width) and (min-aspect-ratio)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: CSS-only

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Grid template-columns varying by aspect-ratio media queries
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Discrete column count changes at aspect-ratio boundaries
layout_strategy.content_priority: All photos shown; density adapts
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Wide+short viewports show more thumbnails simultaneously

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap thumbnail opens detail; swipe through gallery
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same plus pinch-to-zoom for grid density (gesture)
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover shows quick actions; click opens detail
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Arrow keys navigate grid, Enter opens

performance_notes: Pure CSS grid — no JS. sizes attribute reflects actual rendered width based on orientation+breakpoint — browser downloads correctly sized images. aspect-square reservation prevents CLS.
accessibility_across_viewports: ul/li structure for screen readers. Alt text on each image. Real focusable links wrap each thumbnail (not shown in snippet). Arrow key navigation via roving tabindex (advanced).
edge_cases_handled: Ultrawide 21:9 monitor — 8 column grid via min-aspect-ratio query
Phone in landscape — 5 columns instead of treating 812 as 'tablet portrait'
iPad in Split View — width changes but orientation stays, grid adapts to width
User zooms 200% — aspect ratios still meaningful, grid stays useful
Foldable Fold in tablet mode — wide+square, uses landscape-style columns
what_breaks_naive_approach: Width-based breakpoints treat 812x375 (phone landscape) the same as 768x1024 (tablet portrait) — same column count, but landscape phone has 25% the vertical space, showing way fewer photos. Combining width + aspect-ratio gives a better density per orientation.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Aspect ratio drives column density — landscape gets more, portrait gets fewer. 2) sizes attribute uses orientation-aware queries — browser picks correct image resolution. 3) Multiple aspect-ratio breakpoints (2/1 for super-ultrawide) prevent thumbnails from getting too small.

title: Split-view-aware app shell: iPad Stage Manager and Split View at arbitrary widths
viewport_spectrum: iPad in Split View 320-694px, Stage Manager arbitrary 320-1366px, iPad full screen 1180px landscape, iPad in Slide Over (compact)
scenario_context: An app needs to work whether the iPad is fullscreen (full layout), Split View (half-width feels like phone), or Stage Manager (window can be any size 320-1366). Width-based queries handle this — but the user must understand the layout is RESPONDING to their resize, not stuck on 'iPad' template.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: UA-sniffing for iPad (Stage Manager not detectable that way)
Locking layout to physical device class
Hard-binding 768px = tablet (Stage Manager at 700px should feel like phone)
Resize observers on body that re-render entire app
responsive_reasoning: 1. Stage Manager makes 'iPad' arbitrary in width. Treat physical device as irrelevant; respond purely to current viewport width.\n2. Combined with pointer:coarse and hover detection for input-model awareness.\n3. matchMedia handles resize transitions automatically.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Current viewport width + pointer type
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: matchMedia + useSyncExternalStore (per responsive-001)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Cookie-based hint preferred
detection_strategy.code_pattern: useBreakpoint() // returns mobile/tablet/desktop based on width only

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Width-driven adaptive layout, ignoring device type
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Standard breakpoints at 768/1280
layout_strategy.content_priority: Same content; layout adapts based on currently-available width
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Stage Manager at 700px = phone density; at 1100px = tablet density; at 1300px = desktop density

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Single pane (whether physical phone or iPad in narrow window)
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Two pane (whether physical iPad or Stage Manager window in mid range)
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Three pane (whether physical desktop or Stage Manager wide)
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Same; iPad with keyboard works in any layout

performance_notes: matchMedia fires only on breakpoint crossing — no per-pixel work during resize. Cookie write is throttled by React effect (only on actual change). No layout thrash.
accessibility_across_viewports: Resize indicator uses role=status + aria-live=polite (non-disruptive screen reader announcement). All three layouts (mobile/tablet/desktop) have full a11y as shown in responsive-001.
edge_cases_handled: User drags Stage Manager window from 1100px to 600px — matchMedia fires at 768 crossing, layout adapts from tablet to mobile
Slide Over compact (320px width) — falls into mobile layout despite being on iPad
Multi-window via Split View — each window has independent layout
Window resize during data fetch — layout swap doesn't cancel fetches (queries are global)
User rotates iPad in Split View — both halves get new widths
what_breaks_naive_approach: UA sniffing returns 'iPad' for Stage Manager at 600px window — app shows tablet two-pane layout in 600px width, panes are 240px each, unusable. Width-only matchMedia is the only correct signal: device identity is irrelevant.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Device-class agnostic — Stage Manager, Split View, Slide Over all 'just work'. 2) Subtle resize indicator helps users understand the system is responding. 3) Cookie persistence means SSR matches current state.

title: Inspector panel that collapses to a sheet on narrow viewports without losing state
viewport_spectrum: 1440px+ (inspector always open, fixed width), 1024-1439px (inspector toggleable, side), 768-1023px (inspector toggleable, overlay), <768px (inspector as bottom sheet)
scenario_context: A design tool has a properties inspector showing currently-selected element's attributes. On wide screens it's a permanent right rail. On narrow screens it's hidden behind a toggle. When the user selects a new element, the inspector should reveal automatically on small screens; on wide screens it just updates content.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Always-mounted inspector with display:none on small (a11y duplication)
Different inspector components per viewport (state drift)
Inspector that loses scroll position when toggled
Modal inspector on tablet that blocks the canvas
responsive_reasoning: 1. Same inspector component, three visual contexts: rail, overlay, sheet. State (which element selected, scroll position) preserved across.\n2. Use CSS custom properties / classes to switch container behavior; component logic identical.\n3. Auto-reveal on selection only on narrow viewports (intentional user intent on wide).

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport class for inspector chrome
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: useBreakpoint
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default closed on server

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Same React component, container mode varies
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Categorical for container; fluid for content
layout_strategy.content_priority: Always preserves the inspector's data; chrome changes
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Mobile sheet may show more compact spacing

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap selected element → sheet rises from bottom showing inspector; swipe down to dismiss
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Toggle button in top bar opens overlay from right; backdrop dismisses
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Inspector always visible (or toggleable via 'I' shortcut)
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Cmd+I toggles inspector; Tab navigates within inspector

performance_notes: Content unmounts only when inspector fully closed on desktop (w-0). On tablet/mobile, portal-based render avoids deep tree updates. Scroll position preserved manually via ref.
accessibility_across_viewports: Desktop: aria-label on aside. Tablet/mobile: role=dialog aria-modal. Cmd+I shortcut for power users. Focus management not shown but should trap focus when dialog mode.
edge_cases_handled: User selects element while inspector closed on desktop — inspector stays closed (intentional)
User on mobile selects element — sheet auto-rises
User resizes from desktop to mobile while inspector open — content stays, mode changes
Inspector scroll position restored after re-open
Multiple selections in rapid succession — only one open animation
what_breaks_naive_approach: Different components per viewport drift in state (e.g., expanded sections in tree don't survive resize). Always-mounted hidden inspector duplicates aria roles confusing screen readers. Auto-reveal on every selection (including desktop) is annoying for power users.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same children, three chrome modes — state lives in children, not container. 2) Auto-reveal selectively on small viewports only. 3) Scroll position preserved across mode transitions.

title: Horizontal scroll table with sticky first column and sticky header for dense data on narrow viewports
viewport_spectrum: 375px (sticky first col + horizontal scroll), 768px (more cols, less scroll), 1280px (full width, no scroll)
scenario_context: A financial dashboard table has 'Ticker' column followed by 12 columns of daily prices. On mobile, hiding columns loses data. Collapsing to cards loses tabular comparison. The right answer: keep table layout, sticky the Ticker column on horizontal scroll so user always sees which row they're reading.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: overflow-x: auto without sticky column (user loses row context)
position: sticky on td without proper z-index (header overlaps)
Sticky that breaks on iOS (older versions)
Scroll-snap that fights with native momentum
responsive_reasoning: 1. Tabular data has TWO axes — column (varies horizontally) and row (vertical). Losing one axis (row identity) when scrolling horizontally is a UX failure.\n2. Sticky first column + sticky header creates a fixed reference frame.\n3. Border-shadow indicates when content scrolls beneath sticky column (visual scroll affordance).

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Container width vs total table width
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: container queries on table wrapper, scroll position via JS for shadow indicator
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure CSS for sticky; JS-enhanced shadow
detection_strategy.code_pattern: .cell-sticky-col { position: sticky; left: 0; z-index: 2; background: white; }

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS position:sticky on row header column + thead, with shadow on scroll
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Continuous — sticky works at any width
layout_strategy.content_priority: All columns preserved; horizontal scroll reveals overflow
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Same data; sticky column maintains row context during scroll

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe horizontally to reveal columns; ticker column sticky
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same; more columns visible without scroll at 768+
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Shift+wheel for horizontal scroll; cursor changes over scrollable region
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab through cells; Home/End jump to first/last cell

performance_notes: Pure CSS sticky positioning — GPU accelerated. Single scroll listener with passive: true. tabular-nums for aligned price digits. border-separate enables sticky cells to have proper backgrounds without border bleed.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real <table> with scope='col' on column headers, scope='row' on row headers. Screen readers announce 'Ticker AAPL, 2024-01-15, $182.50, +1.2%' when navigating. Sticky doesn't change semantic structure.
edge_cases_handled: iOS momentum scroll — sticky stays sticky during fling
Very wide table (50 columns) — horizontal scroll just works, ticker column always visible
Vertical scroll with both header and first col sticky — z-index ordering keeps header on top at the intersection
RTL languages — sticky right column instead (requires class swap)
Tab navigation — cell focus auto-scrolls into view
what_breaks_naive_approach: overflow-x: auto without sticky column means user scrolls 5 columns right, can no longer see which ticker the row belongs to. They scroll left to check, lose their column position. Tabular comparison is impossible. Sticky first column preserves row identity throughout horizontal navigation.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Sticky first column preserves row context during horizontal scroll — critical for tabular reading. 2) Box-shadow shadow on sticky column appears only when scrolled — visual affordance. 3) Works at every viewport — table just becomes more scrollable on narrow ones.

title: Multi-step form with progress indicator that adapts: stepper on desktop, dots on mobile, percentage in keyboard focus
viewport_spectrum: 375px (dots), 768px (compact stepper with labels), 1024px+ (full stepper with descriptions)
scenario_context: A 5-step onboarding form. Desktop shows a stepper at the top with step numbers, names, and descriptions. Mobile has no space for that — show dots or a percentage bar. Each step's form layout also adapts (single column mobile, two column desktop where appropriate).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Same stepper component squished on mobile (unreadable)
Hiding step names on mobile entirely (no context)
Progress bar with no a11y label
Forms that lose state on viewport change
responsive_reasoning: 1. Stepper communicates 'where you are' + 'where you've been' + 'where you're going'. At desktop, fully verbose. At mobile, abstracted to position only.\n2. Form layout within each step varies — name+email side-by-side on desktop, stacked on mobile.\n3. State (current step, filled values) persists across viewport changes.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Viewport for stepper variant
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: useBreakpoint + container queries on form
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default mobile stepper SSR; hydrate to detected

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Adaptive stepper component + container queries on form fields
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Stepper is categorical; fields are fluid with container query breakpoints
layout_strategy.content_priority: Always: current step. Mobile: position. Desktop: full context.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Stepper hides step descriptions on mobile, shows on desktop

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap Next button at bottom (fixed). Stepper not clickable (no room).
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Stepper clickable on previously-completed steps
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Stepper fully clickable on previously-completed steps, hover shows step description
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab through fields, Enter submits, stepper buttons are tabbable

performance_notes: Container queries for stepper variant selection — zero JS. Form state lives in parent (preserved across viewport changes). Single React tree renders all three stepper variants but only one is visible (acceptable trade-off for state simplicity).
accessibility_across_viewports: Mobile: role=progressbar with aria-valuenow/min/max. All variants: aria-current=step on current. Button-based steppers are keyboard accessible. Disabled future steps are aria-disabled.
edge_cases_handled: User completes step 3 and navigates back to step 1 — step 2 still clickable (already completed)
Resize from desktop to mobile mid-form — current step + values preserved
Screen reader navigation — progressbar reads 'Step 3 of 5: Personal info', stepper variants are aria-hidden when not displayed (via container query)
Long step label on mobile dots view — truncates, full text in description below
Keyboard user tabs to disabled future step button — gets skipped naturally
what_breaks_naive_approach: Same stepper everywhere is unreadable on mobile (5 buttons + labels + descriptions in 375px = chaos). Hidden stepper on mobile leaves user with no progress context. Just a percentage bar with no label provides no semantic meaning.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Three distinct stepper visualizations matching available space. 2) Form fields adapt via container queries — same field component works in any width. 3) State preserved across all transitions — viewport change doesn't reset progress.

title: Background video that swaps to image on slow connections and respects prefers-reduced-motion + reduced-data
viewport_spectrum: Fast desktop 1920px (full video), slow 3G mobile (image fallback), reduced-motion users (static image), Save-Data header users
scenario_context: Hero section with autoplay background video. On fast connections and large screens it's atmospheric. On slow connections it eats data and delays LCP. Reduced motion users get nausea. The video must be opt-in via signals: connection speed, prefers-reduced-motion, Save-Data header.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Always-loading 4MB video (terrible on cellular)
Video without poster (black until loaded)
Ignoring prefers-reduced-motion (accessibility violation)
Video on mobile that breaks landscape orientation
Auto-pause on tab blur not implemented (battery drain)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Network conditions vary more than viewports. Same 1920px viewport could be on Gigabit or 3G.\n2. navigator.connection.effectiveType ('slow-2g'|'2g'|'3g'|'4g') + saveData flag drive decision.\n3. prefers-reduced-motion always disables video. prefers-reduced-data also.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Connection quality, motion preference, data preference
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: navigator.connection + matchMedia(prefers-reduced-motion) + matchMedia(prefers-reduced-data)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default to image SSR; upgrade to video on client after checks

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Conditional video/image swap with image as default (graceful degradation)
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Network-driven, not viewport-driven
layout_strategy.content_priority: Image always present (poster + fallback). Video enhances if conditions allow.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Same visual story; motion is bonus

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Image only on cellular; video on WiFi if motion allowed
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same logic
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Video if connection good
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: No interaction needed (decorative); accessible alt text

performance_notes: Image is the LCP candidate — loaded eagerly with priority. Video preload=metadata downloads only headers initially. Video element only mounts when conditions met (no wasted RAM). Pause on tab hidden saves CPU/battery. saveData and connection.effectiveType prevent burning cellular data.
accessibility_across_viewports: Video is aria-hidden (decorative). Image has alt text for screen reader / video fallback users. prefers-reduced-motion respected — no surprise motion. Content overlays use sufficient contrast (4.5:1).
edge_cases_handled: User on 4G with saveData=true (data saver mode) — image only, video skipped
User on iOS with low power mode — video may not autoplay, falls back to poster gracefully
User toggles reduced-motion mid-session — useReducedMotion fires, video could be unmounted (further enhancement)
Slow connection that becomes fast — connection.change fires, video upgrades
User in fullscreen mode → exits — video continues seamlessly
what_breaks_naive_approach: Autoplaying 4MB video on 3G mobile burns user's data, delays LCP by 8 seconds, fails Core Web Vitals. Ignoring prefers-reduced-motion is a documented accessibility violation. Not pausing on tab blur drains battery on background tabs.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Network quality is responsive context, not just viewport. 2) Three signals must all align (network, motion, data) for video to play — graceful degradation by default. 3) Battery preservation via visibility pause.

title: Scroll-driven nav: hides on scroll down, reveals on scroll up, accounting for soft keyboard and overscroll
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (hide nav on scroll), tablet (same), desktop (always visible). Mobile with keyboard summoned (keep hidden). iOS overscroll bounce (don't trigger reveal).
scenario_context: Long article pages need maximum reading space. Sticky top nav is wasteful on mobile. Hide it on scroll down, reveal on scroll up — matches iOS Safari and Chrome. Naive implementations trigger on overscroll bounce, conflict with soft keyboard, and feel jittery.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Comparing scrollY to previous scrollY without threshold (jittery)
Listening to every scroll event without throttle (performance)
Hiding nav when keyboard appears (visualViewport scroll fires)
iOS overscroll bounce triggers hide/show
Hiding when at top of page (overscroll above 0)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Scroll-driven UX is mobile-first. Desktop has room to spare; keep nav visible.\n2. Direction detection via scroll delta (with threshold to ignore overscroll bounce).\n3. iOS Safari address bar already does this for native chrome; coexist.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Scroll direction with hysteresis, viewport class for enable/disable
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: scroll event with rAF, scrollY delta, useBreakpoint
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default visible on server; hide-on-scroll behavior is client-only
detection_strategy.code_pattern: const delta = scrollY - lastY; if (Math.abs(delta) > 8) { setHidden(delta > 0); lastY = scrollY; }

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Sticky top nav with transform translate-y based on scroll state
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Enable/disable based on viewport class; otherwise continuous
layout_strategy.content_priority: Content priority increases — nav yields space when user is consuming
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Effective viewport gains the nav's height while reading

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Scroll down → nav slides up (out). Scroll up (any) → reveals. Touch + drag at top of page → no hide.
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same as mobile or always-visible (configurable)
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Always visible (mouse scroll isn't a strong signal)
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Page Down/Up don't trigger hide (key-driven scroll, not gesture)

performance_notes: Single rAF-throttled scroll listener. Passive: true tells browser scroll doesn't preventDefault — better scroll perf. Transform-based hide is GPU-accelerated. Threshold of 8px ignores overscroll bounce wiggles. minScroll of 80px prevents hide when user is near top of page.
accessibility_across_viewports: Nav is a real <header> with semantic landmark. When hidden, still keyboard-accessible (transform doesn't affect tab order). User scrolling up reveals nav immediately if they need it.
edge_cases_handled: iOS overscroll bounce at top — minScroll 80 prevents false hide
User taps input, keyboard appears, visualViewport changes (window.scrollY may not change) — nav doesn't hide unexpectedly
User on desktop — feature disabled, nav always visible (mouse wheel scroll isn't strong intent signal)
User scrolls down slowly with reading scroll — small deltas below threshold don't trigger hide
Programmatic scrollTo — fires scroll events, may flicker but settles in <200ms
what_breaks_naive_approach: scrollY > prevScrollY without threshold flickers on iOS overscroll bounce — nav appears and disappears as user reaches bottom and rubber-bands back. Listening to every scroll event without rAF burns CPU. Enabling on desktop is unexpected — users don't expect chrome to auto-hide on a Mac.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Threshold + minScroll handle iOS overscroll bounce correctly. 2) Disabled on desktop where it would be unexpected. 3) Transform-based hide preserves keyboard accessibility (nav still in tab order, just translated).

title: Foldable device support: Galaxy Fold and Pixel Fold with hinge-aware layout via CSS Spanning API
viewport_spectrum: Galaxy Z Fold5 unfolded 1812x2176 (with 90° hinge in middle), Pixel Fold unfolded 1840x2208, both folded states (~280-380px wide), Surface Duo dual-screen
scenario_context: A reading app on a Galaxy Fold unfolded shows content with a vertical hinge bisecting the screen — text under the hinge is partially obscured. CSS Spanning API (in Edge/Chromium foldables) provides env(fold-top), env(fold-left), env(fold-width), env(fold-height). Layout should split content into two halves with the fold as a natural gutter.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single column spanning across the fold (text obscured)
Treating fold as just 'big tablet' (loses opportunity)
Hard-coding device detection (other foldables coming)
Ignoring folded state (280px viewport!)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Foldables are a NEW viewport class. Hinge creates a physical divider that should align with layout boundaries.\n2. Fold posture (folded/half-open/unfolded) and orientation (book/laptop) drive layout.\n3. CSS Spanning API is the standard signal. Fallback to width-based detection for unsupported browsers.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Fold position via CSS env(), screen spanning state
detection_strategy.how_to_detect: @media (horizontal-viewport-segments: 2) or (vertical-viewport-segments: 2) + env(viewport-segment-width 0 0)
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default single-pane; client enhancement when foldable detected

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS grid with column gap aligned to fold using viewport-segment env() variables
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Categorical when fold present; fluid otherwise
layout_strategy.content_priority: Left segment: main content. Right segment: TOC, related, or page 2 of the same content.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Foldables show TWO panes of content with hinge as natural divider

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Folded state = single-column phone UX
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Half-open laptop posture = top reading area, bottom controls
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Unfolded = two-column book mode
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Bluetooth keyboard with unfolded works as a small laptop

performance_notes: Pure CSS for layout — no JS during normal use. Foldable detection uses native CSS env(). Posture changes trigger media query updates only when hinge state changes (rare).
accessibility_across_viewports: Two-panel layout uses real article + aside landmarks. Tab order is left segment first, then right (natural reading order). Touch targets remain 44pt in either segment.
edge_cases_handled: Folded state (280px) — falls back to single column phone layout
Half-open laptop posture (vertical fold) — content above, controls below
Unsupported browser (Safari, Firefox without flag) — falls back to single column, fully usable
Surface Duo with two separate screens — env() variables describe two segments
User rotates foldable — horizontal fold becomes vertical fold, media query updates
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single column running across the hinge has text physically obscured by the fold crease — partial words. Ignoring foldables entirely treats a 1812px-wide screen as 'desktop' with the hinge bisecting the layout awkwardly. CSS Spanning API is the only correct primitive.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Hinge-aware layout uses physical screen properties as design constraints. 2) Graceful fallback for non-foldable browsers via single-column default. 3) Both horizontal and vertical fold orientations handled (laptop posture, book posture).
